title: Netflix

infobox.value: Worldwide (except China, Crimea, North Korea, Russia, and Syria)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Russia_suspension-4)
* [Streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") * [video on demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") * [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution")
* Film production * film distribution * television production * television distribution
August 29, 1997; 28 years ago (1997-08-29) (as a DVD e-commerce) January 16, 2007; 19 years ago (2007-01-16) (as a streaming service)

is an American [subscription video on-demand](/wiki/Subscription_video_on-demand "Subscription video on-demand") [over-the-top](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_service "Over-the-top media service") [streaming](/wiki/Streaming "Streaming") service. The service primarily distributes original and [acquired](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") [films](/wiki/Film "Film") and [television shows](/wiki/Television_show "Television show") from various genres. It is available internationally in multiple languages.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

**Netflix** is an American [subscription video on-demand](/wiki/Subscription_video_on-demand "Subscription video on-demand") [over-the-top](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_service "Over-the-top media service") [streaming](/wiki/Streaming "Streaming") service. The service primarily distributes original and [acquired](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") [films](/wiki/Film "Film") and [television shows](/wiki/Television_show "Television show") from various genres. It is available internationally in multiple languages.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Launched in 2007, nearly a decade after [Netflix, Inc.](/wiki/Netflix,_Inc. "Netflix, Inc.") began its pioneering [DVD-by-mail](/wiki/DVD-by-mail "DVD-by-mail") [movie rental](/wiki/Movie_rental "Movie rental") service, Netflix is the [most-subscribed video on demand](/wiki/List_of_streaming_media_services#Streaming_video_on_demand "List of streaming media services") [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") service, with 325 million paid memberships in more than 190 countries as of 2026.[[7]](#cite_note-300million-7)[[8]](#cite_note-whitten-8) By 2022, "Netflix Original" productions accounted for half of its library in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the namesake company had ventured into other categories, such as [video game publishing](/wiki/Video_game_publishing "Video game publishing") of [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") through its flagship service. As of 2025, Netflix is the [18th most-visited website in the world](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_websites "List of most-visited websites"), with 21.18% of its traffic coming from the US, followed by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") at 6.01%, [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") at 4.94%, and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") at 4.24%.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Netflix](/wiki/Timeline_of_Netflix "Timeline of Netflix").

### Launch as a mail-based rental business (1997–2006)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Launch as a mail-based rental business (1997–2006)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Marc_Randolph_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Marc_Randolph_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marc_Randolph_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

[Marc Randolph](/wiki/Marc_Randolph "Marc Randolph"), co-founder of Netflix and the first CEO of the company

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Reed_Hastings%2C_Web_2.0_Conference.jpg/250px-Reed_Hastings%2C_Web_2.0_Conference.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reed_Hastings,_Web_2.0_Conference.jpg)

[Reed Hastings](/wiki/Reed_Hastings "Reed Hastings"), co-founder and executive chairman

Netflix logo history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Netflix_first_logo.svg/250px-Netflix_first_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_first_logo.svg)

Logo used from 1997 to 2000

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Netflix_%282000%29.svg/250px-Netflix_%282000%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_(2000).svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Netflix_logo.svg/250px-Netflix_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_logo.svg)

Logo used from 2001 to 2014

Netflix was founded by [Marc Randolph](/wiki/Marc_Randolph "Marc Randolph") and [Reed Hastings](/wiki/Reed_Hastings "Reed Hastings") on August 29, 1997, in [Scotts Valley, California](/wiki/Scotts_Valley,_California "Scotts Valley, California"). Hastings, a computer scientist and mathematician, was a co-founder of [Pure Software](/wiki/Pure_Software "Pure Software"), which was acquired by [Rational Software](/wiki/Rational_Software "Rational Software") that year for $750 million, the then-biggest acquisition in [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") history.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Randolph had worked as a marketing director for Pure Software after Pure Atria acquired a company where Randolph worked. He was previously a co-founder of [MicroWarehouse](/wiki/MicroWarehouse "MicroWarehouse"), a computer mail-order company, as well as vice president of marketing for [Borland](/wiki/Borland "Borland").[[12]](#cite_note-started-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Hastings and Randolph came up with the idea for Netflix while [carpooling](/wiki/Carpool "Carpool") between their homes in [Santa Cruz, California](/wiki/Santa_Cruz,_California "Santa Cruz, California"), and Pure Atria's headquarters in Sunnyvale.[[14]](#cite_note-Netflixed-14) [Patty McCord](/wiki/Patty_McCord "Patty McCord"), later head of [human resources](/wiki/Human_resources "Human resources") at Netflix, was also in the carpool group.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Randolph admired [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") and wanted to find a large category of portable items to sell over the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") using a similar model. Hastings and Randolph considered and rejected selling and renting [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS"), as they were too expensive to stock and too delicate to ship.[[12]](#cite_note-started-12) When they heard about DVDs, first introduced in the US in early 1997, they tested the concept of [online DVD rental](/wiki/DVD-by-mail "DVD-by-mail") or sales by mail, by mailing a [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") to Hastings's house in Santa Cruz.[[12]](#cite_note-started-12) When the CD arrived intact, they decided to enter the $16 billion [home-video](/wiki/Home-video "Home-video") sales and rental industry.[[12]](#cite_note-started-12)[[14]](#cite_note-Netflixed-14)[[a]](#cite_note-17) Hastings invested $2.5 million into Netflix from the sale of Pure Atria.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[14]](#cite_note-Netflixed-14) Netflix launched as the first DVD rental and sales website with 30 employees and 925 titles available—nearly all DVDs published.[[14]](#cite_note-Netflixed-14)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Randolph and Hastings met with [Jeff Bezos](/wiki/Jeff_Bezos "Jeff Bezos"), whose Amazon offered to acquire Netflix for between $14 and $16 million. Fearing competition from Amazon, Randolph thought the offer was fair, but Hastings, who owned 70% of the company, turned it down.[[20]](#cite_note-chaos-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Not restricted to the size of a retail store, Netflix could offer hundreds of thousands of DVDs to customers in a [long tail](/wiki/Long_tail "Long tail") business model. Early employee Cindy Holland compared the company acquiring DVDs to "shoveling coal in the side door of the house".[[16]](#cite_note-tavlin2025winter-16) Initially, Netflix offered a per-rental model for each DVD but introduced a monthly subscription concept in September 1999.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The per-rental model was dropped by early 2000, allowing the company to focus on the business model of flat-fee unlimited rentals without due dates, late fees, shipping and handling fees, or per-title rental fees.[[23]](#cite_note-24) While customers enjoyed the convenience, more important to Netflix was that it did not have to store or ship DVDs; subscribers' homes acted as warehouses. The company secretly slowed deliveries to heavy users ("pigs"), to discourage them from quickly returning DVDs.[[16]](#cite_note-tavlin2025winter-16)

In September 2000, during the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble"), while Netflix was suffering losses, Hastings and Randolph offered to sell the company to [Blockbuster](/wiki/Blockbuster_(retailer) "Blockbuster (retailer)") for $50 million. [John Antioco](/wiki/John_Antioco "John Antioco"), CEO of Blockbuster, thought the offer was a joke and declined, saying, "The dot-com hysteria is completely overblown."[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) While Netflix experienced fast growth in early 2001, the continued effects of the dot-com bubble collapse and the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") caused the company to delay plans for its [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") (IPO) and to lay off one-third of its 120 employees.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Netflixenvelope.jpg/250px-Netflixenvelope.jpg)](/wiki/File:Netflixenvelope.jpg)

Opened Netflix rental envelope containing a DVD copy of *[Coach Carter](/wiki/Coach_Carter "Coach Carter")* (2005)

DVD players were a popular gift for holiday sales in late 2001, and demand for DVD subscription services were "growing like crazy", according to chief talent officer McCord.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The company went [public](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") on May 23, 2002, opening on [NASDAQ](/wiki/NASDAQ "NASDAQ") at $15.00 per share with a sale of 5.5 million shares of [common stock](/wiki/Common_stock "Common stock").[[28]](#cite_note-29) In 2003, Netflix was issued a patent by the [US Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office") to cover its subscription rental service and several extensions.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The company posted its first profit in 2003, earning $6.5 million on revenues of $272 million; by 2004, profit had increased to $49 million on over $500 million in revenues.[[30]](#cite_note-nbcnews_lowersubs_2007-31) In 2005, 35,000 different films were available, and Netflix shipped 1 million DVDs out every day.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In 2004, Blockbuster introduced a DVD rental service that allowed users to check out titles through online sites and return them at [brick-and-mortar](/wiki/Brick-and-mortar "Brick-and-mortar") stores.[[32]](#cite_note-cnbc_blockbuster_war-33) By 2006, Blockbuster's service reached two million users, and while trailing Netflix's subscriber count, was drawing business away from Netflix. Netflix lowered fees in 2007.[[30]](#cite_note-nbcnews_lowersubs_2007-31) While it was an [urban legend](/wiki/Urban_legend "Urban legend") that Netflix ultimately "killed" Blockbuster in the DVD rental market, Blockbuster's debt load and internal disagreements hurt the company.[[32]](#cite_note-cnbc_blockbuster_war-33)

In April 2006, Netflix filed a [patent infringement](/wiki/Patent_infringement "Patent infringement") lawsuit in which it demanded a [jury trial](/wiki/Jury_trial "Jury trial") in the [United States District Court for the Northern District of California](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_California "United States District Court for the Northern District of California"), alleging that Blockbuster's online DVD rental subscription program violated two patents held by Netflix. The first [cause of action](/wiki/Cause_of_action "Cause of action") alleged Blockbuster's infringement of copying the "dynamic queue" of DVDs available for each customer, Netflix's method of using the ranked preferences in the queue to send DVDs to subscribers, and Netflix's method permitting the queue to be updated and reordered.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The second cause of action alleged infringement of the subscription rental service as well as Netflix's methods of communication and delivery.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The companies settled their dispute in June 2007; terms were not disclosed.[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

In October 2006,[[39]](#cite_note-40) Netflix announced the [Netflix Prize](/wiki/Netflix_Prize "Netflix Prize"): $1million to the first developer of a video-recommendation [algorithm](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") that could beat its existing algorithm,[[40]](#cite_note-auto1-41) Cinematch, at predicting customer ratings by more than 10%. In September 2009, it awarded the $1 million prize to a seven-person team, "BellKor's Pragmatic Chaos".[[41]](#cite_note-42) Cinematch, launched in 2000, was a system that recommended movies to its users, many of which might have been entirely new to the user.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) As of April 2012, Netflix had not used the $1 million algorithm and did not have plans to implement it due to engineering costs.[[40]](#cite_note-auto1-41)

Through its [Red Envelope Entertainment](/wiki/Red_Envelope_Entertainment "Red Envelope Entertainment") division, Netflix licensed and distributed independent films such as *[Born into Brothels](/wiki/Born_into_Brothels "Born into Brothels")* (2004) and *[Sherrybaby](/wiki/Sherrybaby "Sherrybaby")* (2006). In late 2006, Red Envelope Entertainment also expanded into producing original content with filmmakers such as [John Waters](/wiki/John_Waters "John Waters").[[44]](#cite_note-45) Netflix closed Red Envelope Entertainment in 2008.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### Transition to streaming services (2007–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Transition to streaming services (2007–2012)")]

Hastings reportedly told [Mynette Louie](/wiki/Mynette_Louie "Mynette Louie") in the late-1990s that his goal was always [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"), and that Netflix rented DVDs only to grow its customer base for streaming.[[16]](#cite_note-tavlin2025winter-16) By the mid-2000s, data speeds and bandwidth costs improved sufficiently, allowing customers to download movies from the internet. The original idea was a "Netflix box" that could download movies overnight and be ready to watch the next day. By 2005, Netflix had acquired movie rights and designed the box and service. But after witnessing how popular streaming services such as [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") were despite the lack of high-definition content, the concept of using a hardware device was scrapped and replaced with a streaming concept.[[47]](#cite_note-48) In January 2007, the company launched a streaming media service, introducing [video on demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") via the Internet. However, at that time, it only had 1,000 films available for streaming, compared to 70,000 available on DVD.[[48]](#cite_note-netfx-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) The service, then called "Watch Now", at first required [Internet Explorer](/wiki/Internet_Explorer "Internet Explorer") on a computer. Hollywood studios (including [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox"), [Sony Pictures](/wiki/Sony_Pictures "Sony Pictures"), [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM"), [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros."), [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema"), and [Lionsgate](/wiki/Lionsgate_Films "Lionsgate Films")) licensed [second-run](/wiki/Second-run "Second-run") content to the service, not expecting it to threaten their existing lucrative relationships with [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television").[[16]](#cite_note-tavlin2025winter-16)[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In February 2007, Netflix delivered its billionth DVD, a copy of *[Babel](/wiki/Babel_(film) "Babel (film)")* (2006) to a customer in Texas.[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53) In April 2007, Netflix recruited [ReplayTV](/wiki/ReplayTV "ReplayTV") founder [Anthony Wood](/wiki/Anthony_Wood_(businessman) "Anthony Wood (businessman)"), to build a "Netflix Player" that would allow streaming content to be played directly on a television rather than a desktop or laptop.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Hastings eventually shut down the project to help encourage other hardware manufacturers to include built-in Netflix support, which would be spun off as the [digital media player](/wiki/Digital_media_player "Digital media player") product [Roku](/wiki/Roku "Roku").[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-Fastcompany-57)

In January 2008, all rental-disc subscribers became entitled to unlimited streaming at no additional cost. This change came in response to the introduction of [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") and to [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s new video-rental services.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In August 2008, the Netflix database was corrupted and the company was unable to ship DVDs to customers for three days, leading the company to move all its data to the [Amazon Web Services](/wiki/Amazon_Web_Services "Amazon Web Services") cloud.[[59]](#cite_note-60) In November 2008, Netflix began offering subscribers rentals on [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") and discontinued its sale of used DVDs.[[60]](#cite_note-61) In 2009, Netflix streams overtook DVD shipments.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

In January 2010, Netflix agreed with [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") to delay new release rentals to 28 days after the DVDs became available for sale, in an attempt to help studios sell physical copies, and similar deals involving [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") and [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") were reached on April 9.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) In February 2010, Netflix signed a deal to stream 300 indie movie titles.[[65]](#cite_note-66) In July 2010, Netflix signed a deal to stream movies of [Relativity Media](/wiki/Relativity_Media "Relativity Media").[[66]](#cite_note-67) It also announced an expansion of its deal with [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television") adding more shows, including *[Veronica Mars](/wiki/Veronica_Mars "Veronica Mars")* and *[Nip/Tuck](/wiki/Nip/Tuck "Nip/Tuck")*.[[67]](#cite_note-68) In August 2010, Netflix reached a five-year deal worth nearly $1 billion to stream films from [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [Lionsgate](/wiki/Lionsgate_Films "Lionsgate Films"), and [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"). The deal increased Netflix's annual spending fees, adding roughly $200 million per year. It spent $117 million in the first six months of 2010 on streaming, up from $31 million in 2009.[[68]](#cite_note-69) In September 2010, Netflix launched in Canada, its first international market.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71) In November 2010, Netflix began offering a standalone streaming service separate from DVD rentals.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In 2010, Netflix acquired the rights to *[Breaking Bad](/wiki/Breaking_Bad "Breaking Bad")*, produced by Sony Pictures Television, after the show's third season, at a point where original broadcaster [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") had expressed the possibility of canceling the show. Sony pushed Netflix to release *Breaking Bad* in time for the fourth season, which as a result, greatly expanded the show's audience on AMC due to new viewers binging on past episodes via Netflix, and doubling its viewership by the time of the fifth season. *Breaking Bad* is considered the first show to have this "Netflix effect".[[72]](#cite_note-73)

In January 2011, Netflix announced agreements with several manufacturers to include [branded Netflix buttons](/wiki/Netflix_button "Netflix button") on the [remote controls](/wiki/Remote_control "Remote control") of devices compatible with the service, such as [Blu-ray players](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray").[[73]](#cite_note-74) By May 2011, Netflix had become the largest source of Internet streaming traffic in North America, accounting for 30% of traffic during peak hours.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)

In July 2011, Netflix announced it would be separating its existing subscription plans into two: one covering the streaming and the other DVD rental services.[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-hikes-80) The cost for streaming would be $7.99 per month, while DVD rental would start at the same price.[[80]](#cite_note-fire-81) In September 2011, Netflix expanded to countries in Latin America.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) That same month, Netflix announced its intentions to rebrand and restructure its DVD home media rental service as an independent subsidiary called Qwikster, separating DVD rental and streaming services.[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89) Customers reacted negatively, seeing this as a price increase and a betrayal of customer loyalty. Netflix's stock value dropped, and 800,000 of its 12 million customers cancelled their subscriptions. Netflix quickly apologized, and in October 2011, announced it would retain its DVD service under the name Netflix and that its streaming and DVD-rental plans would remain branded together.[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)

On September 26, 2011, Netflix announced a content deal with [DreamWorks Animation](/wiki/DreamWorks_Animation "DreamWorks Animation").[[92]](#cite_note-93)

In October 2011, Netflix and [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW") signed a multi-year output deal for its television shows.[[93]](#cite_note-94) In January 2012, Netflix started its expansion to Europe, launching in the UK and Ireland.[[94]](#cite_note-95) In February 2012, Netflix reached a multi-year agreement with [The Weinstein Company](/wiki/The_Weinstein_Company "The Weinstein Company").[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97) In March 2012, Netflix acquired the [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name") DVD.com.[[97]](#cite_note-98) By 2016, Netflix rebranded its DVD-by-mail service under the name *DVD.com, A Netflix Company*.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) In April 2012, Netflix filed with the [Federal Election Commission](/wiki/Federal_Election_Commission "Federal Election Commission") (FEC) to form a [political action committee](/wiki/Political_action_committee "Political action committee") (PAC) called FLIXPAC.[[100]](#cite_note-FLIXPAC-101) Netflix spokesperson Joris Evers tweeted that the intent was to "engage on issues like [net neutrality](/wiki/Net_neutrality "Net neutrality"), [bandwidth caps](/wiki/Bandwidth_cap "Bandwidth cap"), [UBB](/wiki/Usage-based_billing "Usage-based billing") and [VPPA](/wiki/VPPA "VPPA")".[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) In June 2012, Netflix signed a deal with [Open Road Films](/wiki/Open_Road_Films "Open Road Films").[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105)

In August 2012, Netflix and [The Weinstein Company](/wiki/The_Weinstein_Company "The Weinstein Company") signed a multi-year output deal for RADiUS-TWC films.[[105]](#cite_note-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107) In September 2012, [Epix](/wiki/Epix "Epix") signed a five-year streaming deal with Netflix. For the initial two years of this agreement, first-run and back-catalog content from Epix was exclusive to Netflix. Epix films came to Netflix 90 days after premiering on Epix.[[107]](#cite_note-108) These included films from Paramount, Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer, and Lionsgate.[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)

In October 2012, Netflix launched in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").[[110]](#cite_note-111)[[111]](#cite_note-112) In December 2012, Netflix and Disney announced an exclusive multi-year agreement for first-run US subscription television rights to [Walt Disney Studios](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(division) "Walt Disney Studios (division)")' animated and live-action films, with classics such as *[Dumbo](/wiki/Dumbo "Dumbo")* (1941), *[Alice in Wonderland](/wiki/Alice_in_Wonderland_(1951_film) "Alice in Wonderland (1951 film)")* (1951)*,* and *[Pocahontas](/wiki/Pocahontas_(1995_film) "Pocahontas (1995 film)")* (1995) available immediately and others available on Netflix beginning in 2016.[[112]](#cite_note-113) [Direct-to-video](/wiki/Direct-to-video "Direct-to-video") releases were made available in 2013.[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115)

In January 2013, Netflix signed an agreement with [Time Warner](/wiki/WarnerMedia "WarnerMedia")'s [Turner Broadcasting System](/wiki/Turner_Broadcasting_System "Turner Broadcasting System") and Warner Bros. Television to distribute [Cartoon Network](/wiki/Cartoon_Network "Cartoon Network"), [Warner Bros. Animation](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Animation "Warner Bros. Animation"), and [Adult Swim](/wiki/Adult_Swim "Adult Swim") content, as well as [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)")'s *[Dallas](/wiki/Dallas_(2012_TV_series) "Dallas (2012 TV series)")*, beginning in March 2013. The rights to these programs were given to Netflix shortly after deals with [Viacom](/wiki/Viacom_(2005%E2%80%932019) "Viacom (2005–2019)") to stream [Nickelodeon](/wiki/Nickelodeon "Nickelodeon") and [Nick Jr. Channel](/wiki/Nick_Jr._Channel "Nick Jr. Channel") programs expired.[[115]](#cite_note-116)

For cost reasons, Netflix stated it would limit its expansion in 2013,[[116]](#cite_note-117) adding only one new market—the Netherlands—in September of that year. This expanded its availability to 40 territories.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)

### Development of original programming and distribution expansion (2013–2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development of original programming and distribution expansion (2013–2017)")]

Netflix had long closely analyzed its customers' preferences. Watch Now gave the company real-time data on their behavior, such as scenes that customers replayed or skipped, or when they stopped watching a show.[[16]](#cite_note-tavlin2025winter-16) In 2011, Netflix began its efforts into original content development. In March, it made a straight-to-series order from [MRC](/wiki/MRC_(company) "MRC (company)") for the political drama [*House of Cards*](/wiki/House_of_Cards_(American_TV_series) "House of Cards (American TV series)"), led by [Kevin Spacey](/wiki/Kevin_Spacey "Kevin Spacey"), outbidding US cable networks. This marked the first instance of a first-run television series being specifically commissioned by the service.[[119]](#cite_note-120) Netflix executives said that its customers' love of films by Spacey and show director [David Fincher](/wiki/David_Fincher "David Fincher") caused the company to acquire the show. Customers' tendency to [binge watch](/wiki/Binge_watch "Binge watch") many episodes without stopping caused it to release all 13 episodes of the show's first season at the same time.[[16]](#cite_note-tavlin2025winter-16) In November the same year, Netflix added two more significant productions to its roster: the comedy-drama *[Orange Is the New Black](/wiki/Orange_Is_the_New_Black "Orange Is the New Black")* (*OITNB*), adapted from [Piper Kerman](/wiki/Piper_Kerman "Piper Kerman")'s [memoir](/wiki/Orange_Is_the_New_Black:_My_Year_in_a_Women%27s_Prison "Orange Is the New Black: My Year in a Women's Prison"),[[120]](#cite_note-121) and a new season of the previously canceled Fox sitcom *[Arrested Development](/wiki/Arrested_Development "Arrested Development")*.[[121]](#cite_note-netflix_revival-122) Netflix acquired the US rights to the Norwegian drama *[Lilyhammer](/wiki/Lilyhammer "Lilyhammer")* after its television premiere on Norway's [NRK1](/wiki/NRK1 "NRK1") in January 2012. Notably departing from the traditional broadcast television model of weekly episode premieres, Netflix chose to release the entire first season on February 8 of the same year.[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[123]](#cite_note-Greene,_Andy-124)

*House of Cards* was released by Netflix on February 1, 2013, marketed as the first "Netflix Original" production.[[124]](#cite_note-125) Later that month, Netflix announced an agreement with [DreamWorks Animation](/wiki/DreamWorks_Animation "DreamWorks Animation") to commission children's television series based on its properties, beginning with *[Turbo: F.A.S.T.](/wiki/Turbo:_F.A.S.T. "Turbo: F.A.S.T.")*, a spin-off of its film *[Turbo](/wiki/Turbo_(2013_film) "Turbo (2013 film)")* (2013)*.*[[125]](#cite_note-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127) *OITNB* would premiere in July 2013; Netflix stated that *OITNB* had been its most-watched original series so far, with all of them having "an audience comparable with successful shows on cable and broadcast TV."[[127]](#cite_note-128)[[128]](#cite_note-129)

In March 2013, Netflix added a [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") sharing feature, letting US subscribers access "Watched by your friends" and "Friends' Favorites" by agreeing.[[129]](#cite_note-130) This was not legal until the [Video Privacy Protection Act](/wiki/Video_Privacy_Protection_Act "Video Privacy Protection Act") was modified in early 2013.[[130]](#cite_note-131) In August 2013, Netflix reintroduced the "Profiles" feature that permits accounts to house up to five user profiles.[[131]](#cite_note-132)[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)[[134]](#cite_note-135)

In November 2013, [Marvel Television](/wiki/Marvel_Television "Marvel Television") and [ABC Studios](/wiki/ABC_Studios "ABC Studios") announced Netflix had ordered a slate of [four television series](/wiki/Marvel%27s_Netflix_television_series "Marvel's Netflix television series") based on the [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics") characters [Daredevil](/wiki/Daredevil_(Marvel_Comics_character) "Daredevil (Marvel Comics character)"), [Jessica Jones](/wiki/Jessica_Jones "Jessica Jones"), [Iron Fist](/wiki/Iron_Fist_(character) "Iron Fist (character)"), and [Luke Cage](/wiki/Luke_Cage "Luke Cage"). Each of the four series received an initial order of 13 episodes, and Netflix also ordered a [Defenders](/wiki/Defenders_(comics) "Defenders (comics)") miniseries that would tie them together. [*Daredevil*](/wiki/Daredevil_(TV_series) "Daredevil (TV series)") and [*Jessica Jones*](/wiki/Jessica_Jones_(TV_series) "Jessica Jones (TV series)") premiered in 2015.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-Netflix-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138) The *[Luke Cage](/wiki/Luke_Cage_(TV_series) "Luke Cage (TV series)")* series premiered in September 2016, followed by *[Iron Fist](/wiki/Iron_Fist_(TV_series) "Iron Fist (TV series)")* in March 2017, and *[The Defenders](/wiki/The_Defenders_(miniseries) "The Defenders (miniseries)")* in August 2017.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140) Marvel owner [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney") later entered into other content agreements with Netflix, including acquiring its animated *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* series *[Star Wars: The Clone Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Clone_Wars "Star Wars: The Clone Wars")*, and a new sixth season.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[Audio logo](/wiki/File:Netflix_audio_logo_(2019).webm "File:Netflix audio logo (2019).webm")

Netflix's [audio logo](/wiki/Audio_logo "Audio logo"), used since 2014

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In February 2014, Netflix began to enter into agreements with US internet service providers, beginning with [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast") (whose customers had repeatedly complained of frequent [buffering](/wiki/Data_buffer "Data buffer") when streaming Netflix), to provide the service a direct connection to their networks.[[141]](#cite_note-142)[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144) In April 2014, Netflix signed *[Arrested Development](/wiki/Arrested_Development "Arrested Development")* creator [Mitchell Hurwitz](/wiki/Mitchell_Hurwitz "Mitchell Hurwitz") and his production firm The Hurwitz Company to a multi-year deal to create original projects for the service.[[144]](#cite_note-145) In May 2014, Netflix and [Sony Pictures Animation](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Animation "Sony Pictures Animation") had a major multi-deal to acquire streaming rights to produce films.[[145]](#cite_note-146) It also began to introduce an updated logo, with a flatter appearance and updated typography.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

In September 2014, Netflix expanded into six new European markets, including [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland").[[147]](#cite_note-148) On September 10, 2014, Netflix participated in [Internet Slowdown Day](/wiki/Internet_Slowdown_Day "Internet Slowdown Day") by deliberately slowing down its speed in support of [net neutrality](/wiki/Net_neutrality "Net neutrality") regulations in the US.[[148]](#cite_note-149) In October 2014, Netflix announced a four-film deal with [Adam Sandler](/wiki/Adam_Sandler "Adam Sandler") and his [Happy Madison Productions](/wiki/Happy_Madison_Productions "Happy Madison Productions").[[149]](#cite_note-150)

In April 2015, following the launch of *[Daredevil](/wiki/Daredevil_(TV_series) "Daredevil (TV series)")*, Netflix director of content operations Tracy Wright announced that Netflix had added support for [audio description](/wiki/Audio_description "Audio description"), and had begun to work with its partners to add descriptions to its other original series over time.[[150]](#cite_note-151)[[151]](#cite_note-152) The following year, as part of a settlement with the [American Council of the Blind](/wiki/American_Council_of_the_Blind "American Council of the Blind"), Netflix agreed to provide descriptions for its original series within 30 days of their premiere, and add [screen reader](/wiki/Screen_reader "Screen reader") support and the ability to browse content by availability of descriptions.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

In March 2015, Netflix expanded to [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155) In September 2015, Netflix launched in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), its first country in Asia.[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158) In October 2015, Netflix launched in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), and [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").[[158]](#cite_note-159)

In January 2016, at the [Consumer Electronics Show](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Show "Consumer Electronics Show"), Netflix announced a major international expansion of its service into 130 additional countries, making it available worldwide except in [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), and [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea").[[159]](#cite_note-160) As part of this expansion, Netflix officially launched its services in Africa, with a focus on [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria").[[160]](#cite_note-auto-161) Between 2018 and 2020, Netflix had started making significant investments in African storytelling, and hired [Dorothy Ghettuba](/wiki/Dorothy_Ghettuba "Dorothy Ghettuba"), a Kenyan media entrepreneur, as head of African Originals.[[161]](#cite_note-162) According to the company, it has invested the equivalent of €160 million in film content production in Africa since it began working on the continent in 2016, with over 12,000 jobs created across Nigeria, Kenya, and South Africa.[[160]](#cite_note-auto-161)

In April 2016, Hastings stated that the company planned to expand its in-house, Los Angeles-based Netflix Studios to grow its output; Hastings ruled out any potential acquisitions of existing studios.[[162]](#cite_note-163) In May 2016, Netflix created a tool called Fast.com to determine the speed of an Internet connection.[[163]](#cite_note-164) It received praise for being "simple" and "easy to use", and does not include [online advertising](/wiki/Online_advertising "Online advertising"), unlike competitors.[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[165]](#cite_note-166)[[166]](#cite_note-167) In November 2016, Netflix launched an offline playback feature, allowing users of the Netflix mobile apps on [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") or [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") to [cache](/wiki/Cache_(computing) "Cache (computing)") content on their devices in standard or high quality for viewing offline, without an Internet connection.[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) Netflix released an estimated 30 original series or films over that year, more than any network or cable channel.[[171]](#cite_note-Backlash-172)

In February 2017, Netflix signed a [music publishing](/wiki/Music_publishing "Music publishing") deal with [BMG Rights Management](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management"), whereby BMG would oversee rights outside of the US for music associated with Netflix Original content. Netflix continues to handle these tasks in-house in the US.[[172]](#cite_note-173) In April 2017, Netflix signed a licensing deal with [IQIYI](/wiki/IQIYI "IQIYI"), a Chinese video streaming platform owned by [Baidu](/wiki/Baidu "Baidu"), to allow selected Netflix Original content to be distributed in China on the platform.[[173]](#cite_note-iQiyi-174)[[174]](#cite_note-175)

In August 2017, Netflix acquired [Millarworld](/wiki/Millarworld "Millarworld"), the creator-owned publishing company of comic book writer [Mark Millar](/wiki/Mark_Millar "Mark Millar"). The purchase marked the first corporate acquisition to have been made by Netflix.[[175]](#cite_note-Millarworld-176) That same month, Netflix entered into an exclusive development deal with [Shonda Rhimes](/wiki/Shonda_Rhimes "Shonda Rhimes") and her production company [Shondaland](/wiki/Shondaland "Shondaland").[[176]](#cite_note-177)

In September 2017, Netflix announced it would offer its low-broadband mobile technology to airlines to provide better in-flight Wi-Fi so passengers could watch movies on Netflix while on planes.[[177]](#cite_note-178) That same month, [Minister of Heritage](/wiki/Minister_of_Heritage "Minister of Heritage") [Mélanie Joly](/wiki/M%C3%A9lanie_Joly "Mélanie Joly") announced that Netflix had agreed to make a [CA$](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar")500 million (US$400 million) investment over the next five years in producing content in Canada. The company denied that the deal was intended to result in a [tax break](/wiki/Tax_break "Tax break").[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[179]](#cite_note-180) Netflix realized this goal by December 2018.[[180]](#cite_note-181)

In October 2017, Netflix iterated a goal of having half of its library consist of original content by 2019, announcing a plan to invest $8 billion on original content in 2018.[[181]](#cite_note-182) In October 2017, Netflix introduced the "Skip Intro" feature, which allows customers to skip the intros to shows on its platform through a variety of techniques including manual reviewing, audio tagging, and [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning").[[182]](#cite_note-183)[[183]](#cite_note-184)

In November 2017, Netflix signed an exclusive multi-year deal with *Orange Is the New Black* creator [Jenji Kohan](/wiki/Jenji_Kohan "Jenji Kohan").[[184]](#cite_note-185) That same month, Netflix withdrew from co-hosting a party at the [75th Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/75th_Golden_Globe_Awards "75th Golden Globe Awards") with [The Weinstein Company](/wiki/The_Weinstein_Company "The Weinstein Company") due to the [Harvey Weinstein sexual abuse cases](/wiki/Harvey_Weinstein_sexual_abuse_cases "Harvey Weinstein sexual abuse cases").[[185]](#cite_note-186)

### Expansion into international productions and new productions, Co-CEOs (2017–2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Expansion into international productions and new productions, Co-CEOs (2017–2020)")]

See also: [International expansion of Netflix](/wiki/International_expansion_of_Netflix "International expansion of Netflix")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Netflix_2016_N_logo.svg/250px-Netflix_2016_N_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_2016_N_logo.svg)

Icon used since 2016

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Netflix_at_Thong_Lor.jpg/250px-Netflix_at_Thong_Lor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Netflix_at_Thong_Lor.jpg)

Netflix advertising at [Thong Lo BTS station](/wiki/Thong_Lo_BTS_station "Thong Lo BTS station"), Bangkok

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Netflix_booth_%2836079051696%29.jpg/250px-Netflix_booth_%2836079051696%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Netflix_booth_(36079051696).jpg)

Netflix's booth at the 2017 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con")

In November 2017, Netflix announced it would be making its first original Colombian series, to be executive produced by [Ciro Guerra](/wiki/Ciro_Guerra "Ciro Guerra").[[186]](#cite_note-de_la_Fuente-187) In December 2017, Netflix signed *[Stranger Things](/wiki/Stranger_Things "Stranger Things")* director-producer [Shawn Levy](/wiki/Shawn_Levy "Shawn Levy") and his production company [21 Laps Entertainment](/wiki/21_Laps_Entertainment "21 Laps Entertainment") to what sources say is a four-year deal.[[187]](#cite_note-SANDBERG-188) In 2017, Netflix invested in distributing exclusive stand-up comedy specials from [Dave Chappelle](/wiki/Dave_Chappelle "Dave Chappelle"), [Louis C.K.](/wiki/Louis_C.K. "Louis C.K."), [Chris Rock](/wiki/Chris_Rock "Chris Rock"), [Jim Gaffigan](/wiki/Jim_Gaffigan "Jim Gaffigan"), [Bill Burr](/wiki/Bill_Burr "Bill Burr"), and [Jerry Seinfeld](/wiki/Jerry_Seinfeld "Jerry Seinfeld").[[188]](#cite_note-Luckerson-189)

In February 2018, Netflix acquired the rights to *[The Cloverfield Paradox](/wiki/The_Cloverfield_Paradox "The Cloverfield Paradox")* (2018) from [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") for $50 million and launched it on February 4, 2018, shortly after airing its first trailer during [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII"). Analysts believed that Netflix's purchase of the film helped to make the film instantly profitable for Paramount compared to a more traditional theatrical release, while Netflix benefited from the surprise reveal.[[189]](#cite_note-hollywoodreporter1-190)[[190]](#cite_note-thr_cloverfield-191) Other films acquired by Netflix include international distribution for Paramount's *[Annihilation](/wiki/Annihilation_(film) "Annihilation (film)")* (2018)[[190]](#cite_note-thr_cloverfield-191) and Universal's *[News of the World](/wiki/News_of_the_World_(film) "News of the World (film)")* (2020) and worldwide distribution of Universal's *[Extinction](/wiki/Extinction_(2018_film) "Extinction (2018 film)")* (2018),[[191]](#cite_note-McNary-192) Warner Bros.' *[Mowgli: Legend of the Jungle](/wiki/Mowgli:_Legend_of_the_Jungle "Mowgli: Legend of the Jungle")* (2018),[[192]](#cite_note-MowgliNetflix-193) Paramount's *[The Lovebirds](/wiki/The_Lovebirds_(2020_film) "The Lovebirds (2020 film)")* (2020)[[193]](#cite_note-194) and 20th Century Studios' *[The Woman in the Window](/wiki/The_Woman_in_the_Window_(2021_film) "The Woman in the Window (2021 film)")* (2021).[[194]](#cite_note-195) In March, the service ordered *[Formula 1: Drive to Survive](/wiki/Formula_1:_Drive_to_Survive "Formula 1: Drive to Survive")*, a racing docuseries following teams in the [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") world championship.[[195]](#cite_note-motorsportweek-196)

In March 2018, [Sky UK](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK") announced an agreement with Netflix to integrate Netflix's subscription VOD offering into its pay-TV service. Customers with its high-end Sky Q set-top box and service will be able to see Netflix titles alongside their regular Sky channels.[[196]](#cite_note-197) In October 2022, Netflix revealed that its annual revenue from the UK subscribers in 2021 was £1.4bn.[[197]](#cite_note-198)

In April 2018, Netflix pulled out of the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival"), in response to new rules requiring competition films to have been released in [French](/wiki/France "France") theaters. The Cannes premiere of *[Okja](/wiki/Okja "Okja")* in 2017 was controversial, and led to discussions over the appropriateness of films with simultaneous digital releases being screened at an event showcasing theatrical film; audience members also booed the Netflix [production logo](/wiki/Production_logo "Production logo") at the screening. Netflix's attempts to negotiate to allow a limited release in France were curtailed by organizers, as well as French [cultural exception](/wiki/Cultural_exception "Cultural exception") law—where theatrically screened films are legally forbidden from being made available via video-on-demand services until at least 36 months after their release.[[198]](#cite_note-Statt-199)[[199]](#cite_note-Tiffany-200)[[200]](#cite_note-theverge.com-201) Besides traditional Hollywood markets as well as from partners like the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), Sarandos said the company was also looking to expand investments in non-traditional foreign markets due to the growth of viewers outside of North America. At the time, this included programs such as *[Dark](/wiki/Dark_(TV_series) "Dark (TV series)")* from Germany, *[Ingobernable](/wiki/Ingobernable "Ingobernable")* from [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), and *[3%](/wiki/3%25 "3%")* from [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil").[[201]](#cite_note-202)[[202]](#cite_note-203)[[203]](#cite_note-vulture_squid_game-204)

In May 2018, former [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), and his wife, [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama"), signed a deal to produce docuseries, documentaries, and features for Netflix under the Obamas' newly formed production company, [Higher Ground Productions](/wiki/Higher_Ground_Productions "Higher Ground Productions").[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206)

In June 2018, Netflix announced a partnership with [Telltale Games](/wiki/Telltale_Games "Telltale Games") to port its [adventure games](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game") to the service in a streaming video format, allowing simple controls through a television remote.[[206]](#cite_note-207)[[207]](#cite_note-208) The first game, *[Minecraft: Story Mode](/wiki/Minecraft:_Story_Mode "Minecraft: Story Mode")*, was released in November 2018.[[208]](#cite_note-209) In July 2018, Netflix earned the most [Emmy](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") nominations of any network for the first time with 112 nods.[[209]](#cite_note-:0-210) In August 2018, the company signed a five-year exclusive overall deal with international best-selling author [Harlan Coben](/wiki/Harlan_Coben "Harlan Coben").[[210]](#cite_note-211) At the same time, the company signed an overall deal with *[Gravity Falls](/wiki/Gravity_Falls "Gravity Falls")* creator [Alex Hirsch](/wiki/Alex_Hirsch "Alex Hirsch").[[211]](#cite_note-212) In October 2018, Netflix paid under $30 million to acquire [Albuquerque Studios](/wiki/Albuquerque_Studios "Albuquerque Studios") (ABQ Studios), a $91 million film and TV production facility with eight sound stages in [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), for its first US production hub, pledging to spend over $1 billion over the next decade to create one of the largest film studios in North America.[[212]](#cite_note-213)[[213]](#cite_note-214) In November 2018, [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") signed a multi-picture film deal with Netflix, making Paramount the first major film studio to do so.[[214]](#cite_note-215) A sequel to [AwesomenessTV](/wiki/AwesomenessTV "AwesomenessTV")'s *[To All the Boys I've Loved Before](/wiki/To_All_the_Boys_I%27ve_Loved_Before_(film) "To All the Boys I've Loved Before (film)")* (2018) was released on Netflix under the title *[To All the Boys: P.S. I Still Love You](/wiki/To_All_the_Boys:_P.S._I_Still_Love_You "To All the Boys: P.S. I Still Love You")* (2020) as part of the agreement.[[215]](#cite_note-216) In December 2018, the company announced a partnership with [ESPN Films](/wiki/ESPN_Films "ESPN Films") on a [television documentary](/wiki/Television_documentary "Television documentary") chronicling Michael Jordan and the [1997–98 Chicago Bulls season](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Chicago_Bulls_season "1997–98 Chicago Bulls season") titled *[The Last Dance](/wiki/The_Last_Dance_(miniseries) "The Last Dance (miniseries)")*. It was released internationally on Netflix and became available for streaming in the US three months after a broadcast airing on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN").[[216]](#cite_note-217)[[217]](#cite_note-218)

In January 2019, *[Sex Education](/wiki/Sex_Education_(TV_series) "Sex Education (TV series)")* made its debut as a Netflix Original series, receiving much critical acclaim.[[218]](#cite_note-219) On January 22, 2019, Netflix sought and was approved for membership into the [Motion Picture Association of America](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Association_of_America "Motion Picture Association of America") (MPAA), making it the first streaming service to join the association.[[219]](#cite_note-220) In February 2019, *[The Haunting](/wiki/The_Haunting_(TV_series) "The Haunting (TV series)")* creator [Mike Flanagan](/wiki/Mike_Flanagan_(filmmaker) "Mike Flanagan (filmmaker)") joined frequent collaborator Trevor Macy as a partner in [Intrepid Pictures](/wiki/Intrepid_Pictures "Intrepid Pictures") ,and the duo signed an exclusive deal with Netflix to produce television content.[[220]](#cite_note-221) In May 2019, Netflix contracted with [Dark Horse Entertainment](/wiki/Dark_Horse_Entertainment "Dark Horse Entertainment") to make television series and films based on comics from [Dark Horse Comics](/wiki/Dark_Horse_Comics "Dark Horse Comics").[[221]](#cite_note-222) In July 2019, Netflix announced it would be opening a hub at [Shepperton Studios](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios") as part of a deal with [Pinewood Group](/wiki/Pinewood_Group "Pinewood Group").[[222]](#cite_note-223) In early-August 2019, Netflix negotiated an exclusive multi-year film and television deal with *[Game of Thrones](/wiki/Game_of_Thrones "Game of Thrones")* creators and showrunners [David Benioff](/wiki/David_Benioff "David Benioff") and [D.B. Weiss](/wiki/D.B._Weiss "D.B. Weiss").[[223]](#cite_note-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225)[[225]](#cite_note-226)[[226]](#cite_note-227)[[227]](#cite_note-228) The first Netflix production created by Benioff and Weiss was planned as an adaptation of [Liu Cixin](/wiki/Liu_Cixin "Liu Cixin")'s science fiction novel *[The Three-Body Problem](/wiki/The_Three-Body_Problem_(novel) "The Three-Body Problem (novel)")*, part of the *[Remembrance of Earth's Past](/wiki/Remembrance_of_Earth%27s_Past "Remembrance of Earth's Past")* trilogy.[[228]](#cite_note-229) In September 2019, in addition to renewing *Stranger Things* for a [fourth season](/wiki/Stranger_Things_(season_4) "Stranger Things (season 4)"), Netflix signed [The Duffer Brothers](/wiki/The_Duffer_Brothers "The Duffer Brothers") to an [overall deal](/wiki/Overall_deal "Overall deal") covering future film and television projects for the service.[[229]](#cite_note-230)

In November 2019, Netflix and [Nickelodeon](/wiki/Nickelodeon "Nickelodeon") entered into a multi-year agreement to produce several original animated feature films and television series based on Nickelodeon's library of characters. This agreement expanded on their existing relationship, in which new specials based on the past Nickelodeon series *[Invader Zim](/wiki/Invader_Zim "Invader Zim")* and *[Rocko's Modern Life](/wiki/Rocko%27s_Modern_Life "Rocko's Modern Life")* (*[Invader Zim: Enter the Florpus](/wiki/Invader_Zim:_Enter_the_Florpus "Invader Zim: Enter the Florpus")* and *[Rocko's Modern Life: Static Cling](/wiki/Rocko%27s_Modern_Life:_Static_Cling "Rocko's Modern Life: Static Cling"),* respectively) were released by Netflix. Other new projects planned under the team-up include a music project featuring [Squidward Tentacles](/wiki/Squidward_Tentacles "Squidward Tentacles") from the animated television series *[SpongeBob SquarePants](/wiki/SpongeBob_SquarePants "SpongeBob SquarePants")*, and films based on *[The Loud House](/wiki/The_Loud_House "The Loud House")* and *[Rise of the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles](/wiki/Rise_of_the_Teenage_Mutant_Ninja_Turtles "Rise of the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles")*.[[230]](#cite_note-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232)[[232]](#cite_note-233) The agreement with Disney ended in 2019 due to the launch of [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+"), with its Marvel productions moving exclusively to the service in 2022.[[233]](#cite_note-ditch-234)[[234]](#cite_note-Disney+ConfirmDeadline-235) Also in November 2019, Netflix announced it had signed a long-term lease to save the [Paris Theatre](/wiki/Paris_Theater_(Manhattan) "Paris Theater (Manhattan)"), the last single-screen movie theater in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"). The company oversaw several renovations at the theater, including new seats and a concession stand.[[235]](#cite_note-236)[[236]](#cite_note-237)[[237]](#cite_note-238)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/SXSW_2016_-_Ted_Sarandos_%2825752599662%29.jpg/250px-SXSW_2016_-_Ted_Sarandos_%2825752599662%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:SXSW_2016_-_Ted_Sarandos_(25752599662).jpg)

Ted Sarandos, longtime CCO and named co-CEO in 2020

In January 2020, Netflix announced a new four-film deal with Adam Sandler worth up to $275 million.[[238]](#cite_note-239) In February 2020, Netflix formed partnerships with six Japanese creators to produce an original Japanese anime project. This partnership includes manga creator group CLAMP, manga creator [Shin Kibayashi](/wiki/Shin_Kibayashi "Shin Kibayashi"), manga creator Yasuo Ohtagaki, novelist and film director [Otsuichi](/wiki/Otsuichi "Otsuichi"), novelist [Tow Ubukata](/wiki/Tow_Ubukata "Tow Ubukata"), and manga creator [Mari Yamazaki](/wiki/Mari_Yamazaki "Mari Yamazaki").[[239]](#cite_note-240) In March 2020, [ViacomCBS](/wiki/ViacomCBS "ViacomCBS") announced it will be producing two spin-off films based on *SpongeBob SquarePants* for Netflix.[[240]](#cite_note-241) In April 2020, [Peter Chernin](/wiki/Peter_Chernin "Peter Chernin")'s [Chernin Entertainment](/wiki/Chernin_Entertainment "Chernin Entertainment") made a multi-year [first-look deal](/wiki/First-look_deal "First-look deal") with Netflix to make films.[[241]](#cite_note-242) In May 2020, Netflix announced the acquisition of [Grauman's Egyptian Theatre](/wiki/Grauman%27s_Egyptian_Theatre "Grauman's Egyptian Theatre") from the [American Cinematheque](/wiki/American_Cinematheque "American Cinematheque") to use as a special events venue.[[242]](#cite_note-243)[[243]](#cite_note-egyptian-244)[[244]](#cite_note-245) In July 2020, Netflix appointed Sarandos as co-CEO.[[245]](#cite_note-co-CEO_2020-246)[[246]](#cite_note-247) That same month, Netflix invested in *[Black Mirror](/wiki/Black_Mirror "Black Mirror")* creators [Charlie Brooker](/wiki/Charlie_Brooker "Charlie Brooker") and [Annabel Jones](/wiki/Annabel_Jones "Annabel Jones")' new production outfit Broke And Bones.[[247]](#cite_note-Brooker-248)

In September 2020, Netflix signed a multi-million dollar deal with the [Duke](/wiki/Prince_Harry,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex") and [Duchess](/wiki/Meghan,_Duchess_of_Sussex "Meghan, Duchess of Sussex") of Sussex. Harry and Meghan agreed to a multi-year deal promising to create TV shows, films, and children's content as part of their commitment to stepping away from the duties of the royal family.[[248]](#cite_note-249)[[249]](#cite_note-250) That same month, Hastings released a book about Netflix culture titled *No Rules Rules: Netflix and the Culture of Reinvention*, which was coauthored by [Erin Meyer](/wiki/Erin_Meyer "Erin Meyer").[[250]](#cite_note-251) In December 2020, Netflix signed a first-look deal with [Millie Bobby Brown](/wiki/Millie_Bobby_Brown "Millie Bobby Brown") to develop and star in several projects, including a potential action franchise.[[251]](#cite_note-252)

### Expansion into gaming, *Squid Game*, new programing, and new initiatives (2021–2022)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Expansion into gaming, Squid Game, new programing, and new initiatives (2021–2022)")]

In March 2021, Netflix earned the most [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") nominations of any studio, with 36 nods. Netflix won seven Academy Awards, which was the most by any studio.[[252]](#cite_note-253) Later that year, Netflix also won more Emmys than any other network or studio with 44 wins, tying the record for most Emmys won in a single year set by CBS in 1974.[[253]](#cite_note-254)

In April 2021, [Sony Pictures Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Entertainment "Sony Pictures Entertainment") announced an agreement for Netflix to hold the US pay television window rights to its releases beginning in 2022, replacing [Starz](/wiki/Starz "Starz") and expanding upon an existing agreement with [Sony Pictures Animation](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Animation "Sony Pictures Animation"). The agreement also includes a first-look deal for any future direct-to-streaming films being produced by [Sony Pictures](/wiki/Sony_Pictures "Sony Pictures"), with Netflix required to commit to a minimum number of them.[[254]](#cite_note-255)[[255]](#cite_note-256)[[256]](#cite_note-257) On April 27, Netflix announced it was opening its first [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") headquarters in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto").[[257]](#cite_note-258) The company also announced that it would open an office in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), and [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul") to increase its original content in those regions.[[258]](#cite_note-259)

In early-June, Netflix hosted a first-ever week-long virtual event called "Geeked Week", where it shared exclusive news, new trailers, cast appearances, and more about upcoming genre titles like *[The Witcher](/wiki/The_Witcher_(TV_series) "The Witcher (TV series)")*, *[The Cuphead Show!](/wiki/The_Cuphead_Show! "The Cuphead Show!")*, and *[The Sandman](/wiki/The_Sandman_(TV_series) "The Sandman (TV series)")*.[[259]](#cite_note-260) On June 7, [Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez "Jennifer Lopez")'s [Nuyorican Productions](/wiki/Nuyorican_Productions "Nuyorican Productions") signed a multi-year first-look deal with Netflix spanning feature films, TV series, and unscripted content, with an emphasis on projects that support diverse female actors, writers, and filmmakers.[[260]](#cite_note-261) On June 10, Netflix announced it was launching an online store for curated products tied to the Netflix brand and shows such as *Stranger Things* and *The Witcher*.[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-263) On June 21, [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s [Amblin Partners](/wiki/Amblin_Partners "Amblin Partners") signed a deal with Netflix to release multiple new feature films for the streaming service.[[263]](#cite_note-264)[[264]](#cite_note-265) On June 30, [Powerhouse Animation Studios](/wiki/Powerhouse_Animation_Studios "Powerhouse Animation Studios") (the studio behind Netflix's *[Castlevania](/wiki/Castlevania_(TV_series) "Castlevania (TV series)")*) announced signing a first-look deal with the streamer to produce more animated series.[[265]](#cite_note-266)

In July 2021, Netflix hired [Mike Verdu](/wiki/Mike_Verdu "Mike Verdu"), a former executive from [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") and [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), as vice president of game development, along with plans to add video games by 2022.[[266]](#cite_note-267) Netflix announced plans to release [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_games "Mobile games") that would be included in subscribers' service plans.[[267]](#cite_note-268) Trial offerings were first launched for Netflix users in Poland in August 2021, offering premium mobile games based on *Stranger Things,* including *[Stranger Things 3: The Game](/wiki/Stranger_Things_3:_The_Game "Stranger Things 3: The Game")*, for free to subscribers through the Netflix mobile app.[[268]](#cite_note-269) On July 14, 2021, Netflix signed a first-look deal with [Joey King](/wiki/Joey_King "Joey King"), star of *[The Kissing Booth](/wiki/The_Kissing_Booth_(film_series) "The Kissing Booth (film series)")* franchise, in which King would produce and develop films for Netflix via her All The King's Horses production company.[[269]](#cite_note-270) On July 21, [Zack Snyder](/wiki/Zack_Snyder "Zack Snyder"), director of Netflix's *[Army of the Dead](/wiki/Army_of_the_Dead "Army of the Dead")*, announced he had signed his production company [The Stone Quarry](/wiki/The_Stone_Quarry "The Stone Quarry") to a first-look deal with Netflix; his upcoming projects included a sequel to *Army of the Dead* and a sci-fi adventure film titled *[Rebel Moon](/wiki/Rebel_Moon "Rebel Moon")*.[[270]](#cite_note-271)[[271]](#cite_note-272)[[272]](#cite_note-273)[[273]](#cite_note-274) In 2019, he had agreed to produce an [anime-style](/wiki/Anime-influenced_animation "Anime-influenced animation") [web series](/wiki/Web_series "Web series") inspired by [Norse mythology](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology").[[274]](#cite_note-275)[[275]](#cite_note-276)

As of August 2021, [Netflix Originals](/wiki/Netflix_Originals "Netflix Originals") made up 40% of Netflix's overall library in the US.[[276]](#cite_note-277) The company announced that "[TUDUM: A Netflix Global Fan Event](/wiki/Tudum "Tudum")", a three-hour virtual behind the scenes featuring first-look reveals for 100 of the streamer's series, films, and specials, would have its inaugural show in late September 2021.[[277]](#cite_note-278)[[278]](#cite_note-279) According to Netflix, the show garnered 25.7 million views across Netflix's 29 Netflix YouTube channels, Twitter, Twitch, Facebook, TikTok, and Tudum.com.[[279]](#cite_note-280)

Also in September, the company announced The Queen's Ball: A Bridgerton Experience, an immersive, Regency-era ball, launching in 2022 in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[280]](#cite_note-281) Then *[Squid Game](/wiki/Squid_Game "Squid Game")*, a South Korean survival drama created and produced by [Hwang Dong-hyuk](/wiki/Hwang_Dong-hyuk "Hwang Dong-hyuk"), rapidly became the service's most-watched show within a week of its launch in many markets on September 17, 2021, including Korea, the US, and the UK.[[203]](#cite_note-vulture_squid_game-204) Within its first 28 days on the service, *Squid Game* drew more than 111 million viewers, surpassing *[Bridgerton](/wiki/Bridgerton "Bridgerton")* and becoming Netflix's most-watched show.[[281]](#cite_note-282) On September 20, Netflix signed a long-term lease with [Aviva Investors](/wiki/Aviva_Investors "Aviva Investors") to operate and expand the [Longcross Studios](/wiki/Longcross_Studios "Longcross Studios") in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey"), UK.[[282]](#cite_note-283) On September 21, Netflix announced it would acquire the Roald Dahl Story Company, which manages the rights to [Roald Dahl](/wiki/Roald_Dahl "Roald Dahl")'s [stories and characters](/wiki/Roald_Dahl_bibliography "Roald Dahl bibliography"), for an undisclosed price and would operate it as an independent company.[[283]](#cite_note-284)[[284]](#cite_note-285)[[285]](#cite_note-286)[[286]](#cite_note-287) The company acquired [Night School Studio](/wiki/Night_School_Studio "Night School Studio"), an independent video game developer, on September 28.[[287]](#cite_note-288)

On October 13, 2021, Netflix announced the launch of the Netflix Book Club, partnering with [Starbucks](/wiki/Starbucks "Starbucks") for a social series called *But Have You Read the Book?*.[[288]](#cite_note-289) [Uzo Aduba](/wiki/Uzo_Aduba "Uzo Aduba") became inaugural host of the series and announced monthly book selections set to be adapted by the streamer. Aduba speaks with the cast, creators, and authors about the book adaptation process over a cup of coffee at Starbucks.[[289]](#cite_note-290)[[290]](#cite_note-291) Through October 2021, Netflix commonly reported viewership for its programming based on the number of viewers or households that watched a show in a given period (such as the first 28 days from its premiere) for at least two minutes. On the announcement of its quarterly earnings in October 2021, the company stated that it would switch its viewership metrics to measuring the number of hours that a show was watched, including rewatches, which the company said was closer to the measurements used in linear broadcast television, and thus "our members and the industry can better measure success in the streaming world."[[291]](#cite_note-292)

Netflix officially launched mobile games on November 2, 2021, for Android users around the world. Through the app, subscribers had free access to five games, including two previously made *[Stranger Things](/wiki/Stranger_Things "Stranger Things")* titles. Netflix intends to add more games to this service over time.[[292]](#cite_note-293) On November 9, the collection launched for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS").[[293]](#cite_note-294) Some games in the collection require an active internet connection to play, while others will be available offline. Netflix Kids' accounts will not have games available.[[294]](#cite_note-295) On November 16, Netflix announced the launch of "Top10 on Netflix.com", a new website with weekly global and country lists of the most popular titles on their service based on their new viewership metrics.[[295]](#cite_note-spring_tribune-296) On November 22, Netflix announced that it would acquire [Scanline VFX](/wiki/Scanline_VFX "Scanline VFX"), the visual effects and animation company behind *[Cowboy Bebop](/wiki/Cowboy_Bebop_(2021_TV_series) "Cowboy Bebop (2021 TV series)")* and *Stranger Things*.[[296]](#cite_note-297) On the same day, [Roberto Patino](/wiki/Roberto_Patino "Roberto Patino") signed a deal with Netflix and established his production banner, Analog Inc., in partnership with the company. Patino's first project under the deal is a series adaptation of [Image Comics](/wiki/Image_Comics "Image Comics")' *Nocterra*.[[297]](#cite_note-298)

On December 6, 2021, Netflix and [Stage 32](/wiki/Stage_32 "Stage 32") announced that they have teamed up the workshops at the Creating Content for the Global Marketplace program.[[298]](#cite_note-299) On December 7, 2021, Netflix partnered with IllumiNative, a woman-led non-profit organization, for the Indigenous Producers Training Program.[[299]](#cite_note-300)[[300]](#cite_note-301) On December 9, Netflix announced the launch of "[Tudum](/wiki/Tudum#Tudum_(website) "Tudum")", an official companion website that offers news, exclusive interviews and behind-the-scenes videos for its original television shows and films.[[301]](#cite_note-302) On December 13, Netflix signed a multi-year overall deal with [Kalinda Vazquez](/wiki/Kalinda_Vazquez "Kalinda Vazquez").[[302]](#cite_note-303) On December 16, Netflix signed a multi-year creative partnership with [Spike Lee](/wiki/Spike_Lee "Spike Lee") and his production company [40 Acres and a Mule Filmworks](/wiki/40_Acres_and_a_Mule_Filmworks "40 Acres and a Mule Filmworks") to develop film and television projects.[[303]](#cite_note-304)

In compliance with the EU Audiovisual Media Services Directive and its implementation in [France](/wiki/France "France"), Netflix reached commitments with French broadcasting authorities and film guilds, as required by law, to invest a specific amount of its annual revenue into original French films and series. These films must be theatrically released and would not be allowed to be carried on Netflix until 15 months after their release.[[304]](#cite_note-305)[[305]](#cite_note-306)

In January 2022, Netflix ordered additional sports docuseries from *Drive to Survive* producers Box to Box Films, including a series that would follow [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour") golfers (*[Full Swing](/wiki/Full_Swing_(2023_TV_series) "Full Swing (2023 TV series)")*), and another that would follow professional tennis players (*[Break Point](/wiki/Break_Point_(2023_TV_series) "Break Point (2023 TV series)")*).[[306]](#cite_note-307)[[307]](#cite_note-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309)

The company announced plans to acquire Next Games in March 2022 for €65 million as part of Netflix's expansions into gaming. Next Games had developed the mobile title *Stranger Things: Puzzle Tales* as well as two *The Walking Dead* mobile games.[[309]](#cite_note-310) Later that month, Netflix also acquired the [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")-based mobile game developer, [Boss Fight Entertainment](/wiki/Boss_Fight_Entertainment "Boss Fight Entertainment"), for an undisclosed sum.[[310]](#cite_note-variety.com-311)

In March 2022, Netflix announced a partnership with [Dr. Seuss](/wiki/Dr._Seuss "Dr. Seuss") Enterprises to produce five new series and specials based on Seuss [properties](/wiki/Dr._Seuss_bibliography "Dr. Seuss bibliography") following the success of *[Green Eggs and Ham](/wiki/Green_Eggs_and_Ham_(TV_series) "Green Eggs and Ham (TV series)")*.[[311]](#cite_note-312)[[312]](#cite_note-313) On March 29, Netflix announced it would open an office in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") to serve as a hub for its original productions across [Central and Eastern Europe](/wiki/Central_and_Eastern_Europe "Central and Eastern Europe").[[313]](#cite_note-314) On March 30, Netflix extended its lease agreement with Martini Film Studios, just outside [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), for another five years.[[314]](#cite_note-315) On March 31, Netflix ordered a docuseries that would follow teams in the [2022 Tour de France](/wiki/2022_Tour_de_France "2022 Tour de France"), which would also be co-produced by Box to Box Films*.*[[315]](#cite_note-316)

Following the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), Netflix suspended its operations and future projects in Russia.[[316]](#cite_note-317)[[4]](#cite_note-Russia_suspension-4) It also announced that it would not comply with a proposed directive by [Roskomnadzor](/wiki/Roskomnadzor "Roskomnadzor") requiring all internet streaming services with more than 100,000 subscribers to integrate the major free-to-air channels (which are primarily state-owned).[[317]](#cite_note-318) A month later, ex-Russian subscribers filed a class action lawsuit against Netflix.[[318]](#cite_note-319)[[319]](#cite_note-320)

In April 2022, Netflix stated that 100 million households globally were sharing passwords to their account with others, and that Canada and the US accounted for 30 million of them. Following these announcements, Netflix's stock price fell by 35%.[[320]](#cite_note-Kit2022-321)[[321]](#cite_note-FlintJacob2022-322)[[322]](#cite_note-BursztynskyAlessandrini2022-323)[[323]](#cite_note-Sherman2022-324)[[324]](#cite_note-325) By June 2022, Netflix had laid off 450 full-time and contract employees as part of the company's plan to trim costs amid lower than expected subscriber growth.[[325]](#cite_note-326)[[326]](#cite_note-327)[[327]](#cite_note-328)[[328]](#cite_note-329)

On April 13, 2022, Netflix released the series *[Our Great National Parks](/wiki/Our_Great_National_Parks "Our Great National Parks")*, which was hosted and narrated by former US President Barack Obama.[[329]](#cite_note-330) It also partnered with Group Effort Initiative, a company founded by [Ryan Reynolds](/wiki/Ryan_Reynolds "Ryan Reynolds") and [Blake Lively](/wiki/Blake_Lively "Blake Lively"), to provide opportunities behind the camera for those in underrepresented communities.[[330]](#cite_note-331) On the same day, Netflix partnered with Lebanon-based [Arab Fund For Arts And Culture](/wiki/Arab_Fund_for_Arts_and_Culture_(AFAC) "Arab Fund for Arts and Culture (AFAC)") for supporting the Arab female filmmakers. It would provide a one-time grant of $250,000 to female producers and directors in the Arab world through the company's Fund for Creative Equity.[[331]](#cite_note-332) Also on the same day, Netflix announced an *[Exploding Kittens](/wiki/Exploding_Kittens "Exploding Kittens")* mobile card game tied to a new animated TV series, which would launch in May.[[332]](#cite_note-333) Netflix formed a creative partnership with [J. Miles Dale](/wiki/J._Miles_Dale "J. Miles Dale").[[333]](#cite_note-334) The company also formed a partnership with Japan's [Studio Colorido](/wiki/Studio_Colorido "Studio Colorido"), signing a multi-film deal to boost their anime content in Asia. The streaming giant is said to co-produce three feature films with the studio, the first of which would premiere in September 2022.[[334]](#cite_note-335) On April 28, the company launched its inaugural [Netflix Is a Joke](/wiki/Netflix_Is_A_Joke "Netflix Is A Joke") comedy festival, featuring more than 250 shows over 12 nights at 30-plus locations across Los Angeles, including the first-ever stand-up show at [Dodger Stadium](/wiki/Dodger_Stadium "Dodger Stadium").[[335]](#cite_note-336)[[336]](#cite_note-337)

The first volume of *[Stranger Things 4](/wiki/Stranger_Things_4 "Stranger Things 4")* logged Netflix's biggest premiere weekend ever for an original series with 286.79 million hours viewed.[[337]](#cite_note-338) This was preceded by a new *Stranger Things* interactive experience hosted in New York City that was developed by the show's creators.[[338]](#cite_note-339) After the release of the second volume of *Stranger Things 4* on July 1, 2022, it became Netflix's second title to receive more than one billion hours viewed.[[339]](#cite_note-340) On July 19, 2022, Netflix announced plans to acquire Australian animation studio [Animal Logic](/wiki/Animal_Logic "Animal Logic").[[340]](#cite_note-341)[[341]](#cite_note-342) Also that month, in collaboration with [Sennheiser](/wiki/Sennheiser "Sennheiser"), Netflix began to add Ambeo 2-channel audio mixes (referred to as "[spatial audio](/wiki/Spatial_audio "Spatial audio")") to selected original productions, which allows simulated [surround sound](/wiki/Surround_sound "Surround sound") on stereo speakers and headphones.[[342]](#cite_note-Weatherbed2023-343)[[343]](#cite_note-Welch2022-344)

In September 2022, Netflix opened an office in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), Poland, responsible for the service's operations in 28 markets in Central and Eastern Europe.[[344]](#cite_note-345)

In October 2022, Netflix signed a creative partnership with [Andrea Berloff](/wiki/Andrea_Berloff "Andrea Berloff") and [John Gatins](/wiki/John_Gatins "John Gatins").[[345]](#cite_note-346) On October 11, Netflix signed up with the [Broadcasters' Audience Research Board](/wiki/Broadcasters%27_Audience_Research_Board "Broadcasters' Audience Research Board") for external measurement of viewership in the UK.[[346]](#cite_note-347) On October 12, Netflix signed to build a production complex at [Fort Monmouth](/wiki/Fort_Monmouth "Fort Monmouth") in [Eatontown, New Jersey](/wiki/Eatontown,_New_Jersey "Eatontown, New Jersey").[[347]](#cite_note-348) On October 18, Netflix began exploring a cloud gaming offering and opened a new gaming studio in Southern California.[[348]](#cite_note-349)

In November 2022, Netflix announced a strategic partnership with The Seven, a Japanese production company owned by [TBS Holdings](/wiki/TBS_Holdings "TBS Holdings"), to produce multiple original live-action titles over the next five years.[[349]](#cite_note-350)[[350]](#cite_note-351) In December 2022, Netflix announced that 60% of its subscribers had watched a [Korean drama](/wiki/Korean_drama "Korean drama").[[351]](#cite_note-Gruenwedel-352)[[352]](#cite_note-353) CEO Ted Sarandos attributed the increase in viewership of Korean content among Americans to Korean films and dramas being "often unpredictable" and catching "the American audience by surprise".[[353]](#cite_note-Souw-354)[[354]](#cite_note-Park-355)

Inn January 2023, Netflix announced plans to open an engineering hub in its Warsaw office to provide Netflix's creative partners with software solutions for the production of films and series.[[355]](#cite_note-356) In February 2023, Netflix launched a wider rollout of spatial audio, and began allowing Premium subscribers to download content for offline playback on up to six devices (expanded from four).[[342]](#cite_note-Weatherbed2023-343)[[343]](#cite_note-Welch2022-344)

In March 2023, Netflix broadcast its first-ever global live-streaming event, the stand-up comedy special *[Chris Rock](/wiki/Chris_Rock "Chris Rock"): Selective Outrage*.[[356]](#cite_note-357)

Netflix reworked its viewership metrics again in June 2023. Viewership of shows was measured during the first 91 days of availability, instead of the first 28 days, and are now based on the total viewership hours divided by the total hours of the show itself. This provided more equal considerations for shorter shows and movies compared to longer ones.[[357]](#cite_note-358)

In August 2023, the company announced Netflix Stories, a collection of interactive narrative games from Netflix series and movies such as *[Love is Blind](/wiki/Love_Is_Blind_(American_TV_series) "Love Is Blind (American TV series)")*, *[Money Heist](/wiki/Money_Heist "Money Heist"),* and *[Virgin River](/wiki/Virgin_River_(TV_series) "Virgin River (TV series)")*.[[358]](#cite_note-359)

### Discontinuation of DVDs, expansion of live events and venues, WWE (2023–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Discontinuation of DVDs, expansion of live events and venues, WWE (2023–present)")]

In January 2023, Hastings stepped down from his role as co-CEO and assumed the role of executive chairman.
