He was replaced by [Greg Peters](/wiki/Greg_Peters_(businessman) "Greg Peters (businessman)"), who joined [Ted Sarandos](/wiki/Ted_Sarandos "Ted Sarandos") as co-CEOs of Netflix.[[359]](#cite_note-360)[[360]](#cite_note-361) Peters previously served as Chief Operating Officer (COO) and Chief Product Officer (CPO), while Sarandos served as Chief Content Officer (CCO).[[361]](#cite_note-362)[[362]](#cite_note-363)

In April 2023, Netflix announced it would discontinue its [DVD-by-mail](/wiki/DVD-by-mail "DVD-by-mail") service on September 29.[[363]](#cite_note-364) Users of the service were able to keep the DVDs they had received. Over its lifetime, the service had sent out over 5 billion shipments.[[364]](#cite_note-365)[[365]](#cite_note-366)

In October 2023, Eunice Kim was promoted to CPO and Elizabeth Stone was promoted to Chief Technology Officer (CTO).[[366]](#cite_note-367) That same month, amid a restructuring of its animation division, Netflix announced a multi-film agreement with [Skydance Animation](/wiki/Skydance_Animation "Skydance Animation") beginning with *[Spellbound](/wiki/Spellbound_(2024_film) "Spellbound (2024 film)")* (2024). The agreement partially replaces one it had with [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+").[[367]](#cite_note-368)[[368]](#cite_note-369)

In December 2023, Netflix released its first "What We Watched: A Netflix Engagement Report", a look at viewership for every original and licensed title watched more than 50,000 hours from January to June 2023. The company also announced plans to publish the report twice a year.[[369]](#cite_note-370)[[370]](#cite_note-371) In its first report for the first six months of 2023, it revealed that *[The Night Agent](/wiki/The_Night_Agent "The Night Agent")* was the most watched show globally during that period.[[371]](#cite_note-372)

In January 2024, Netflix announced a major agreement with [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") promotion [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE"), under which it would acquire the rights to its live weekly program [*Raw*](/wiki/WWE_Raw "WWE Raw") beginning January 6, 2025; the rights would initially cover the US, Canada, the UK, and Latin America, and expand to other territories over time. As part of the agreement, Netflix also acquired the international rights to WWE's main weekly programs (*Raw*, *[SmackDown](/wiki/SmackDown "SmackDown")*, and *[NXT](/wiki/WWE_NXT "WWE NXT")*) and [pay-per-view events](/wiki/List_of_WWE_pay-per-view_and_livestreaming_supercards "List of WWE pay-per-view and livestreaming supercards") outside of the US, and the former [WWE Network](/wiki/WWE_Network "WWE Network") library of supplemental and archive content. The agreement was reported to be valued at $500 million per-year over ten years.[[372]](#cite_note-373)[[373]](#cite_note-374)[[374]](#cite_note-375) Netflix would later acquire the library rights in the US under a separate deal, replacing an agreement with [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)").[[375]](#cite_note-376)

In February 2024, Netflix joined with [Peter Morgan](/wiki/Peter_Morgan "Peter Morgan"), creator of Netflix series [*The Crown*](/wiki/The_Crown_(TV_series) "The Crown (TV series)"), to produce the play [*Patriots*](/wiki/Patriots_(play) "Patriots (play)") on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"). The venture is the first Broadway credit for the company but not its first stage project. It was actively involved as a producer of *[Stranger Things: The First Shadow](/wiki/Stranger_Things:_The_First_Shadow "Stranger Things: The First Shadow")* in London. Both productions share a lead producer, [Sonia Friedman](/wiki/Sonia_Friedman "Sonia Friedman").[[376]](#cite_note-377)

In May 2024, the company hosted its second Netflix Is a Joke festival in Los Angeles. It streamed several specials from the festival live, including [Katt Williams](/wiki/Katt_Williams "Katt Williams")'s *Woke Folk* and *[The Roast of Tom Brady](/wiki/The_Roast_of_Tom_Brady "The Roast of Tom Brady")*, both of which ranked on Netflix's global top 10 the following two weeks.[[377]](#cite_note-378)[[378]](#cite_note-379) That same month, Netflix announced it would stream both [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") [Christmas games](/wiki/NFL_on_Christmas_Day "NFL on Christmas Day") in 2024.[[379]](#cite_note-380) For 2025 and 2026, the streamer would have exclusive rights to at least one NFL Christmas game each year.[[380]](#cite_note-381)

In June 2024, Netflix announced it would develop new entertainment venues known as "Netflix House" at the [King of Prussia Mall](/wiki/King_of_Prussia_Mall "King of Prussia Mall") in Pennsylvania and [Galleria Dallas](/wiki/Galleria_Dallas "Galleria Dallas") in Texas. The spaces would feature retail shops, restaurants, and other interactive experiences related to Netflix Original content, building upon other "[pop-up](/wiki/Pop-up_retail "Pop-up retail")" initiatives to promote individual programs.[[381]](#cite_note-382) Netflix House Philadelphia would feature immersive experiences for *[Wednesday](/wiki/Wednesday_(TV_series) "Wednesday (TV series)")* and *[One Piece](/wiki/One_Piece_(2023_TV_series) "One Piece (2023 TV series)")*, while Netflix House Dallas will feature *Stranger Things: Escape the Dark* and *Squid Game: Survive the Trials*.[[382]](#cite_note-deadline/1236435263-383) Netflix House Las Vegas Strip will open in 2027.[[382]](#cite_note-deadline/1236435263-383)

In November 2024, Netflix announced it would discontinue further work on interactive specials and remove all but four of them from the platform, citing a desire to focus on "technological efforts in other areas".[[383]](#cite_note-384) On November 15, Netflix streamed a boxing event from [AT&T Stadium](/wiki/AT%26T_Stadium "AT&T Stadium") in [Arlington, Texas](/wiki/Arlington,_Texas "Arlington, Texas"), featuring as co-main events an exhibition match between [Jake Paul and Mike Tyson](/wiki/Jake_Paul_vs._Mike_Tyson "Jake Paul vs. Mike Tyson"), and [Katie Taylor](/wiki/Katie_Taylor "Katie Taylor") versus [Amanda Serrano](/wiki/Amanda_Serrano "Amanda Serrano") for the [WBA](/wiki/List_of_WBA_female_world_champions#Lightweight "List of WBA female world champions"), [WBC](/wiki/List_of_WBC_female_world_champions#Lightweight "List of WBC female world champions"), [IBF](/wiki/List_of_IBF_female_world_champions#Lightweight "List of IBF female world champions"), [WBO](/wiki/List_of_WBO_female_world_champions#Lightweight "List of WBO female world champions"), and [*The Ring*](/wiki/List_of_The_Ring_female_world_champions#Lightweight "List of The Ring female world champions") lightweight titles. While afflicted by technical issues, Paul's promoter reported that the stream had a peak concurrent viewership of 65 million viewers, surpassing the [2023 ICC Men's Cricket World Cup final](/wiki/2023_Cricket_World_Cup_final "2023 Cricket World Cup final") (which had a reported 57 million concurrent streams on [Disney+ Hotstar](/wiki/Disney%2B_Hotstar "Disney+ Hotstar")) as the most live-streamed sporting event.[[384]](#cite_note-385)[[385]](#cite_note-386) Netflix stated that the event had an "average minute audience" (AMA) of 108 million worldwide, and that the AMA of 47 million in the US made the Taylor versus Serrano bout the most-watched women's professional sporting event in US history.[[386]](#cite_note-387)

In December 2024, [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") announced that Netflix would be the exclusive US broadcaster of the [2027](/wiki/2027_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2027 FIFA Women's World Cup") and [2031](/wiki/2031_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2031 FIFA Women's World Cup") [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup"), in what was deemed the platform's most significant push into sports content.[[387]](#cite_note-388) On December 25, Netflix aired its first-ever [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") games: the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") versus the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers"), and the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") versus the [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans"). The games both averaged over 30 million global viewers and became the two most-streamed NFL games in US history, while simultaneously creating Netflix's most-watched Christmas Day ever in the US.[[388]](#cite_note-389)

In January 2025, Netflix announced it had exceeded 300 million subscribers worldwide after adding a record 18.9 million in Q4 of 2024, amounting to 41 million for the full year.[[7]](#cite_note-300million-7) In May 2025, Netflix announced a redesign of its home screen for the first time since 2013, which has a simplified appearance with more prominent metadata, and replaces the sidebar menu with a streamlined section of tabs along the top of the screen. Netflix stated that the redesign was meant to allow users to "have an easier time figuring out if a title is right for them", and be more reflective of new types of content offerings such as sports and other live events.[[389]](#cite_note-390)[[390]](#cite_note-391)

In August 2025, Netflix announced an exclusivity deal to stream the [World Baseball Classic](/wiki/World_Baseball_Classic "World Baseball Classic") in Japan starting in 2026. The 2026 edition marks the first time it will stream a live event in Japan. Through this agreement, Netflix will stream all 47 games of the 2026 World Baseball Classic live and on-demand for its users in Japan.[[391]](#cite_note-392) The animated musical *[KPop Demon Hunters](/wiki/KPop_Demon_Hunters "KPop Demon Hunters")* (2025) became Netflix's most popular film of all time later that month.[[392]](#cite_note-393)

In November 2025, Netflix acquired rights to selected [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) games for the 2026 to 2028 seasons, including [Opening Night](/wiki/Opening_Day "Opening Day"), the [Home Run Derby](/wiki/Home_Run_Derby "Home Run Derby"), and an additional special event matchup each season, including the 2026 season [Field of Dreams game](/wiki/MLB_at_Field_of_Dreams "MLB at Field of Dreams").[[393]](#cite_note-394)

In February 2026, it was announced that two Netflix movies, [Guillermo del Toro](/wiki/Guillermo_del_Toro "Guillermo del Toro")’s *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(2025_film) "Frankenstein (2025 film)")* (2025) and *KPop Demon Hunters,* were being adding to the [Criterion Collection](/wiki/The_Criterion_Collection "The Criterion Collection"), giving the films physical releases.[[394]](#cite_note-395) This first happened in 2020 with[Alfonso Cuarón](/wiki/Alfonso_Cuar%C3%B3n "Alfonso Cuarón")'s *[Roma](/wiki/Roma_(2018_film) "Roma (2018 film)")* (2018),[[395]](#cite_note-396) followed by [Noah Baumbach](/wiki/Noah_Baumbach "Noah Baumbach")'s *[Marriage Story](/wiki/Marriage_Story "Marriage Story")* (2019), [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")* (2019), and a few others.[[396]](#cite_note-397)

### Attempted acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Attempted acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery")]

Further information: [Proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Proposed_acquisition_of_Warner_Bros._Discovery "Proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery")

In October 2025, Netflix, Inc. was reported to be actively exploring a bid for the studios and streaming assets of [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery") (WBD). According to the report, Netflix retained investment bank [Moelis & Co](/wiki/Moelis_%26_Co "Moelis & Co"). to evaluate a potential offer and had been granted access to WBD's financial data room as part of its preparatory due-diligence.[[397]](#cite_note-398) The acquisition would give Netflix control over major IP such as [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") and rights to the *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter")* franchise, along with [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO")'s premium dramas,[[398]](#cite_note-:1-399)[[399]](#cite_note-:2-400) and Warner Bros.' distribution rights of several foreign-produced movies such as *[Under Parallel Skies](/wiki/Under_Parallel_Skies "Under Parallel Skies")* (2024) and *[Ex Ex Lovers](/wiki/Ex_Ex_Lovers "Ex Ex Lovers")* (2025)*,* which were already available on Netflix prior these acquisition deals.[[400]](#cite_note-401)[[401]](#cite_note-402)[[402]](#cite_note-403) Netflix stressed that it remains "predominantly focused on growing organically" and is not interested in owning legacy [linear television](/wiki/Linear_television "Linear television") networks such as [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"), [TBS](/wiki/TBS_(American_TV_channel) "TBS (American TV channel)"), and [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)").[[398]](#cite_note-:1-399)[[399]](#cite_note-:2-400)

On December 4, 2025, Netflix won the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery, to be followed by exclusive talks to finalize a deal.[[403]](#cite_note-404) On December 5, Netflix announced they would be buying the Warner Bros. assets for $72 billion, valuing the entirety of WBD at $82.7 billion. The deal would include the Warner Bros. [film](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Motion_Picture_Group "Warner Bros. Motion Picture Group"), [television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television_Studios "Warner Bros. Television Studios"), and [video game](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Games "Warner Bros. Games") studios, HBO/HBO Max and their respective libraries, [DC Entertainment](/wiki/DC_Entertainment "DC Entertainment")/[DC Studios](/wiki/DC_Studios "DC Studios"), and WBD's distribution and licensing divisions. It would exclude Discovery Global—the planned spin-off of WBD's legacy linear television businesses (including the [Discovery+](/wiki/Discovery%2B "Discovery+") streaming service); pending regulatory approval, the sale and spin-off were expected to both be completed in Q3 of 2026.[[404]](#cite_note-405)

On December 8, 2025, [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance") launched a $77.9 billion [hostile takeover](/wiki/Hostile_takeover "Hostile takeover") of Warner Bros. Paramount argued for its $30 per share all cash offer (versus Netflix's $27.75) and alleged lower regulatory hurdles were better for shareholders.[[405]](#cite_note-406) On December 17, WBD told its shareholders to reject Paramount's bid, instead proceeding with the Netflix acquisition.[[406]](#cite_note-407)

On January 20, 2026, Netflix and Warner Bros. Discovery said they amended the proposed takeover to a new all-cash deal, which removed any uncertainty related to Netflix share price volatility. Warner shareholders were expected to vote on the deal by April.[[407]](#cite_note-408) On February 26, WBD confirmed it considered Paramount's increased bid to be superior to Netflix's current offer, triggering a four-business-day period during which Netflix could improve its offer. Paramount's latest acquisition bid was reported to be approximately $111 billion for the entirety of WBD, including its linear cable channels, for $31 per share.[[408]](#cite_note-409)[[409]](#cite_note-NetflixBowsOut-Variety-410)[[410]](#cite_note-NetflixBacksOut-THR-411) Netflix subsequently declined to increase its bid, in which Sarandos and Greg Peters released a statement saying the deal was "no longer financially attractive."[[411]](#cite_note-412)[[412]](#cite_note-413) This cleared the way for Paramount to pursue its deal.[[413]](#cite_note-414) When asked about a potential acquisition of [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery") in the future on Bloomberg, Ted Sarandos said "Possibly. If you look at the History of Warner Bros."[[414]](#cite_note-415)

### Acquisition of InterPositive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Acquisition of InterPositive")]

On March 5, 2026, it was announced that Netflix was acquiring [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck")'s AI firm, InterPositive, which makes tools developed and intended for filmmaking. The purchase stems from the relationship between the two, with a first-look deal from Artists Equity, the new-model production company led by Affleck and [Matt Damon](/wiki/Matt_Damon "Matt Damon"). In January, the streaming giant released *[The Rip](/wiki/The_Rip_(film) "The Rip (film)")*, an action movie co-starring Affleck and Damon. Affleck first mentioned his AI venture to Netflix execs in the fall of 2025, initiating months of dialogue that culminated in the acquisition.[[415]](#cite_note-:3-416)

The firm will seek to create tools "purpose-built to represent and protect all the qualities that make a great story: the nuances of filmmaking, the predictable – and unpredictable – challenges of production environments, the distortion of a lens or the way light shape-shifts across a scene," Affleck said. "We also need to preserve what makes storytelling human, which is judgment." Affleck will take on the role of senior advisor.[[415]](#cite_note-:3-416)

Netflix CCO Bela Bajaria said, "We believe new tools should expand creative freedom, not constrain it or replace the work of writers, directors, actors, and crews. Ben and his team at InterPositive are part of a long tradition in our industry of artists leading the way in how innovation is used in storytelling."[[415]](#cite_note-:3-416)

### Expansion into podcasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Expansion into podcasts")]

Netflix announced it would expand its catalog to include video podcasts in early 2026.[[416]](#cite_note-417) It acquired the exclusive video podcasting rights from several podcast companies including [IHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia"),[[417]](#cite_note-418) [The Ringer](/wiki/The_Ringer_(website) "The Ringer (website)"), and [Barstool Sports](/wiki/Barstool_Sports "Barstool Sports"). The shows include [The Breakfast Club](/wiki/The_Breakfast_Club_(radio_show) "The Breakfast Club (radio show)"), [Dissect](/wiki/Dissect_(podcast) "Dissect (podcast)") and [Pardon My Take](/wiki/Pardon_My_Take "Pardon My Take").[[418]](#cite_note-419)[[419]](#cite_note-420)

## Availability and access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Availability and access")]

### Global availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Global availability")]

Further information: [International expansion of Netflix](/wiki/International_expansion_of_Netflix "International expansion of Netflix")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Netflix_area.svg/500px-Netflix_area.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_area.svg)

Countries where Netflix is available (as of March 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit)):

Available

Unavailable ([China](/wiki/China "China"),[[420]](#cite_note-421) [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"),[[421]](#cite_note-422) and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria").)

Netflix is available in every country and territory except for [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[422]](#cite_note-423)

In January 2016, Netflix announced it would begin [VPN blocking](/wiki/VPN_blocking "VPN blocking") since it can be used to watch videos from a country where they are unavailable.[[423]](#cite_note-424) The result of the VPN block is that people can only watch videos available worldwide and other videos are hidden from search results.[[424]](#cite_note-425) Variety is present on Netflix.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Hebrew and Arabic's right-to-left interface orientation, which is a common localization strategy in many markets, are what define the Middle Eastern user interface's localization, and in some regions, Netflix offers a more affordable mobile-only subscription.[[425]](#cite_note-426)

### Subscriptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Subscriptions")]

Customers can subscribe to one of three plans; the difference in plans relates to video resolution, the number of simultaneous streams, and the number of devices to which content can be downloaded.[[426]](#cite_note-427)

At the end of Q1 2022, Netflix estimated that 100 million households globally were sharing passwords to their account with others.[[323]](#cite_note-Sherman2022-324) In March 2022, Netflix began to charge a fee for additional users in Chile, Peru, and Costa Rica to attempt to control [account sharing](/wiki/Account_sharing "Account sharing").[[321]](#cite_note-FlintJacob2022-322)[[322]](#cite_note-BursztynskyAlessandrini2022-323)[[323]](#cite_note-Sherman2022-324) On July 18, 2022, Netflix announced that it would test the account sharing feature in more countries, including Argentina, Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras.[[427]](#cite_note-428) On October 17, Netflix launched Profile Transfer to help end account sharing.[[428]](#cite_note-429)

On July 13, 2022, Netflix announced plans to launch an advertising-supported subscription option.[[429]](#cite_note-430) Netflix's planned advertising tier would not allow subscribers to download content like the existing ad-free platform.[[430]](#cite_note-431) On July 20, 2022, it was announced that the advertising-supported tier would be coming to Netflix in 2023 but it would not feature the full library of content.[[431]](#cite_note-432) In October, the launch date was announced as November 3, 2022, and was launched in 12 countries: US, Canada, Mexico, Brazil, UK, France, Germany, [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), Spain, Australia, Japan and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[432]](#cite_note-433)[[433]](#cite_note-434)[[434]](#cite_note-435) The ad-supported plan was called "Basic with Ads" and it cost $6.99 per month in the US at launch.[[435]](#cite_note-436)

On February 24, 2023, Netflix cut subscription prices in more than 30 countries around the world to attract more subscribers from those countries. [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [the Philippines](/wiki/The_Philippines "The Philippines"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") are on the list of countries where the cost for a subscription will be reduced.[[436]](#cite_note-437) In the same month stronger anti-password-sharing rules were expanded to Canada, New Zealand, [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), and [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").[[437]](#cite_note-438) In May 2023, these measures were further expanded to US and Brazil subscribers.[[438]](#cite_note-439)[[439]](#cite_note-440)[[440]](#cite_note-441)

In July 2023, Netflix added 5.9 million subscribers for the second quarter of the year for a total of 238.39 million subscribers overall. The US and Canada accounted for 1.2 million subscribers which was the largest regional quarterly gain since 2021.[[441]](#cite_note-techcrunch723-442)[[442]](#cite_note-443)

In February, Netflix announced it would enforce stricter regulations on password sharing, and by May 2023 it began cracking down in the US, UK, and Australia. Under these new rules, multiple people can use and share one account, but they have to be under the same household. Netflix defines a household as people who live in the same location as the owner of the account. Users are asked to set a primary location based on the device's IP address.[[443]](#cite_note-password_sharing-444)

Netflix reported 8.05 million new subscribers in Q2 2024, up from 5.9 million subscribers added in Q2 2023.[[444]](#cite_note-445)[[445]](#cite_note-446)

In July 2024, Netflix started phasing out its cheapest subscription plan for users in France and the US, a year after the plan was removed for Canada and the UK. Members in these countries have the option to sign up for either the standard ad-free plan or the ad plan.[[446]](#cite_note-447)[[447]](#cite_note-448)

### Device support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Device support")]

Netflix can be accessed via a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"), while Netflix apps are available on various platforms, including [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") players, [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), mobile phones, [smart TVs](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV"), [digital media players](/wiki/Digital_media_player "Digital media player"), and [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"). Currently supported game consoles include:

* Microsoft [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"),[[448]](#cite_note-449) [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One") and [Xbox Series X/S](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X/S "Xbox Series X/S")
* Sony [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3"),[[449]](#cite_note-450) [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") and [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Google_ATV_Reference_RCU_G10_-_Netflix_button.jpg/250px-Google_ATV_Reference_RCU_G10_-_Netflix_button.jpg)](/wiki/File:Google_ATV_Reference_RCU_G10_-_Netflix_button.jpg)

An [Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV")-powered [remote control](/wiki/Remote_control "Remote control") with a [Netflix button](/wiki/Netflix_button "Netflix button")

Several older devices no longer support Netflix. For home gaming consoles, this includes the [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2"), [PlayStation TV](/wiki/PlayStation_TV "PlayStation TV"), [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii")[[450]](#cite_note-451) and [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U").[[451]](#cite_note-452) For handheld gaming consoles, this includes the [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS") family of systems and the [PlayStation Vita](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita "PlayStation Vita").[[452]](#cite_note-453) The second and third generation [Apple TV](/wiki/Apple_TV_(device) "Apple TV (device)") previously supported Netflix with an ad-free plan, but the app was automatically removed on these devices on July 31, 2024.[[453]](#cite_note-454)

In addition, a growing number of [multichannel television](/wiki/Multichannel_television "Multichannel television") providers, including cable television and [IPTV](/wiki/IPTV "IPTV") services, have added Netflix apps accessible within their own [set-top boxes](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box"), sometimes with the ability for its content (along with those of other online video services) to be presented within a unified search interface alongside linear television programming as an "all-in-one" solution.[[454]](#cite_note-455)[[455]](#cite_note-456)[[456]](#cite_note-457)[[457]](#cite_note-458)

The maximum video resolution supported on computers is dependent on the [DRM](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") systems available on a particular operating system and web browser.[[458]](#cite_note-459)

## Content

### Original programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Original programming")]

Further information on Netflix original programming: [Lists of Netflix original television series (both current](/wiki/List_of_Netflix_original_programming "List of Netflix original programming"), [and ended)](/wiki/List_of_ended_Netflix_original_programming "List of ended Netflix original programming"), [original films](/wiki/Lists_of_Netflix_original_films "Lists of Netflix original films"), and [stand-up comedy specials](/wiki/List_of_Netflix_original_stand-up_comedy_specials "List of Netflix original stand-up comedy specials")

Further information on Netflix international offerings: [Lists of Netflix exclusive international distribution programming](/wiki/Lists_of_Netflix_exclusive_international_distribution_programming "Lists of Netflix exclusive international distribution programming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Netflix_Original_Movies.svg/250px-Netflix_Original_Movies.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_Original_Movies.svg)

Netflix Original Movies

"[Netflix Originals](/wiki/Netflix_Originals "Netflix Originals")" are content that is produced, co-produced, or distributed exclusively by Netflix. Netflix funds its original shows differently than other TV networks when they sign a project, providing the money upfront and immediately ordering two seasons of most series.[[462]](#cite_note-463) It keeps licensing rights, which normally give production companies future revenue opportunities from syndication, merchandising, etc.[[171]](#cite_note-Backlash-172)[[463]](#cite_note-464)

Over the years, Netflix output ballooned to a level unmatched by any television network or streaming service. According to *Variety Insight*, Netflix produced a total of 240 new original shows and movies in 2018, then climbed to 371 in 2019, a figure "greater than the number of original series that the entire U.S. TV industry released in 2005."[[464]](#cite_note-465) The Netflix budget allocated to production increased annually, reaching $13.6 billion in 2021 and projected to hit $18.9 billion by 2025, a figure that once again overshadowed any of its competitors.[[465]](#cite_note-466) As of August 2022, original productions made up 50% of Netflix's overall library in the US.[[466]](#cite_note-467)

### Film and television deals

Netflix has exclusive [pay TV](/wiki/Pay_TV "Pay TV") deals with several studios. The deals give Netflix exclusive streaming rights while adhering to the structures of traditional pay TV terms.

Distributors that have licensed content to Netflix include Warner Bros., Universal Pictures, [Sony Pictures Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Entertainment "Sony Pictures Entertainment") and previously The Walt Disney Studios.[[48]](#cite_note-netfx-49) Netflix also holds current and back-catalog rights to television programs distributed by [Walt Disney Television](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Television "Walt Disney Television"), [DreamWorks Classics](/wiki/DreamWorks_Classics "DreamWorks Classics"), [Kino International](/wiki/Kino_International_(company) "Kino International (company)"), [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS"), [Warner Bros. titles from other companies such as [ABS-CBN Studios](/wiki/ABS-CBN_Studios "ABS-CBN Studios"),[[469]](#cite_note-470)[[470]](#cite_note-preview.ph-471) [GMA Network](/wiki/GMA_Network_(company) "GMA Netflix negotiated to distribute animated films from Universal that HBO declined to acquire, such as *[The Lorax](/wiki/The_Lorax_(film) "The Lorax (film)")*, *[ParaNorman](/wiki/ParaNorman "ParaNorman")*, and *[Minions](/wiki/Minions_(film) "Minions (film)")*.[[473]](#cite_note-474)

### Netflix Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Netflix Games")]

In July 2021, Netflix hired Mike Verdu, a former executive from [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") and [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), as vice president of game development, along with plans to add video games by 2022.[[475]](#cite_note-476) Netflix announced plans to release [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_games "Mobile games") that would be included in subscribers' service plans.[[476]](#cite_note-477) Trial offerings were first launched for Netflix users in Poland in August 2021, offering premium mobile games based on *Stranger Things* including *[Stranger Things 3: The Game](/wiki/Stranger_Things_3:_The_Game "Stranger Things 3: The Game")*, for free to subscribers through the Netflix mobile app.[[477]](#cite_note-478)

Netflix officially launched mobile games on November 2, 2021, for Android users around the world. Through the app, subscribers had free access to five games, including two previously made *[Stranger Things](/wiki/Stranger_Things "Stranger Things")* titles. Netflix intends to add more games to this service over time.[[478]](#cite_note-479) On November 9, the collection launched for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS").[[479]](#cite_note-480) Verdu said in October 2022 that besides continuing to expand their portfolio of games, they were also interested in [cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming") options.[[480]](#cite_note-Netflix_cloud_games-481)

To support the games effort, Netflix began acquiring and forming a number of studios. The company acquired [Night School Studio](/wiki/Night_School_Studio "Night School Studio"), an independent video game developer, in September 2021.[[481]](#cite_note-482) Netflix announced plans to acquire Next Games in March 2022 for €65 million as part of Netflix's expansions into gaming. Next Games had developed the mobile title *Stranger Things: Puzzle Tales* as well as two *The Walking Dead* mobile games.[[482]](#cite_note-483) Later in the month, Netflix also acquired the [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")-based mobile game developer, [Boss Fight Entertainment](/wiki/Boss_Fight_Entertainment "Boss Fight Entertainment"), for an undisclosed sum.[[310]](#cite_note-variety.com-311) Netflix opened a mobile game studio in Helsinki, Finland in September 2022,[[483]](#cite_note-484) and a new studio, their fifth overall, in [southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") in October 2022,[[480]](#cite_note-Netflix_cloud_games-481) alongside the acquisition of [Spry Fox](/wiki/Spry_Fox "Spry Fox") in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle").[[484]](#cite_note-485)

In June 2024, Verdu was moved into a new role focusing on "innovation in game development."[[485]](#cite_note-486) The next month, Netflix hired Alain Tascan, vice president of game development at [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games"), to head up Netflix Games.[[486]](#cite_note-487) As of July 2024, Netflix has over 80 games in development, releasing at least one game each month to attract fans.[[487]](#cite_note-488) The company shut down its Southern California "Team Blue" AAA gaming studio in October 2024, leading to the departure of developers like *[Overwatch](/wiki/Overwatch "Overwatch")* producer Chacko Sonny, *[Halo](/wiki/Halo_(series) "Halo (series)")* veteran Joseph Staten and *[God of War](/wiki/God_of_War_(franchise) "God of War (franchise)")* art director Rafael Grassetti.[[488]](#cite_note-489) Netflix indicated that it maintains a commitment to grow its gaming business despite the changes.[[489]](#cite_note-490)[[490]](#cite_note-491) In late October, Netflix announced several games based on hit series including Netflix Stories: Outer Banks, Netflix Stories: A Perfect Couple, Netflix Stories: A Virgin River Christmas, and The Ultimatum: Choices, as well as a new daily word game in partnership with [TED Talks](/wiki/TED_Talks "TED Talks"), TED Tumblewords.[[491]](#cite_note-492)[[492]](#cite_note-493)

On December 12, 2024, at [The Game Awards 2024](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2024 "The Game Awards 2024"), Netflix Games revealed game designer [Yu Suzuki](/wiki/Yu_Suzuki "Yu Suzuki")'s next game *[Steel Paws](/wiki/Steel_Paws "Steel Paws")*.[[493]](#cite_note-494) In November 2025, Netflix released its first batch of TV games which members play by using their phone as a controller. The games included *Boggle Party*, *[Lego Party](/wiki/Lego_Party "Lego Party")*, *[Pictionary](/wiki/Pictionary "Pictionary"): Game Night*, *[Tetris Time Warp](/wiki/Tetris_Time_Warp "Tetris Time Warp")* and *Party Crasher: Fool Your Friends*.[[494]](#cite_note-495)

## Technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Technology")]

### Content delivery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Content delivery")]

Netflix freely [peers](/wiki/Peering "Peering") with [Internet service providers](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider") (ISPs) directly and at common [Internet exchange points](/wiki/Internet_exchange_point "Internet exchange point"). In June 2012, a custom [content delivery network](/wiki/Content_delivery_network "Content delivery network"), [Open Connect](/wiki/Open_Connect "Open Connect"), was announced.[[495]](#cite_note-496) For larger ISPs with over 100,000 subscribers, Netflix offers free *Netflix Open Connect* [computer appliances](/wiki/Computer_appliance "Computer appliance") that cache their content within the ISPs' [data centers](/wiki/Data_center "Data center") or networks to further reduce [Internet transit](/wiki/Internet_transit "Internet transit") costs.[[496]](#cite_note-497)[[497]](#cite_note-498) By August 2016, Netflix closed its last physical data center, but continued to develop its Open Connect technology.[[498]](#cite_note-499) A 2016 study at the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") detected 233 individual Open Connect locations on over six continents, with the largest amount of traffic in the US, followed by Mexico.[[499]](#cite_note-500)[[500]](#cite_note-501)

As of July 2017, Netflix series and movies accounted for more than a third of all prime-time download Internet traffic in North America.[[501]](#cite_note-502)

### API

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: API")]

On October 1, 2008, Netflix offered access to its service via a public [application programming interface](/wiki/Application_programming_interface "Application programming interface") (API).[[502]](#cite_note-503) It allowed access to data for all Netflix titles, and allows users to manage their movie queues. The API was free and allowed commercial use.[[503]](#cite_note-504) In June 2012, Netflix began to restrict the availability of its public API.[[504]](#cite_note-505) Netflix instead focused on a small number of known partners using private interfaces, since most traffic came from those private interfaces.[[505]](#cite_note-506) In November 2014, Netflix retired the public API.[[506]](#cite_note-507) Netflix then partnered with the developers of eight services deemed the most valuable, including Instant Watcher, Fanhattan, Yidio and Nextguide.[[507]](#cite_note-508)

### Recommendations and thumbnails

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Recommendations and thumbnails")]

Netflix presents viewers with recommendations based on interactions with the service, such as previous viewing history and ratings of viewed content. These are often grouped into genres and formats, or feature the platform's highest-rated content. Each title is presented with a thumbnail. Before around 2015, these were the same [key art](/wiki/Key_art "Key art") for everyone, but since then has been customized. Netflix may select a specific key art for a thumbnail based on viewing history,[[508]](#cite_note-509) such as an actor or scene type based on genre preferences.[[509]](#cite_note-510) Some thumbnails are generated from video stills.[[510]](#cite_note-511)

The Netflix recommendation system is a vital part of the streaming platform's success, enabling personalized content suggestions for hundreds of millions of subscribers worldwide.[[511]](#cite_note-RecoAI-512) Using advanced machine learning algorithms, Netflix analyzes user interactions, including viewing history, searches, and ratings, to deliver personalized recommendations for movies and TV shows.

The recommendation system considers individual user preferences, similarities with other users with comparable tastes, specific title attributes (genre, release year, etc.), device usage patterns, and viewing time. As users interact with the platform and provide feedback with their viewing habits, the recommendation system is able to adapt and refine its suggestions over time. Netflix uses a two-tiered ranking system, using the presentation of titles on the homepage for easy navigation to maximize user engagement. This is done by organizing content into rows and ranking the titles within each row based on how much the user would be interested in it.[[511]](#cite_note-RecoAI-512) Netflix also uses A/B testing to determine what causes the biggest interest and engagement related to options concerning movie suggestions and how titles are organized.

Tags like "bittersweet", "sitcom", or "intimate" are assigned to each title by Netflix employees.[[512]](#cite_note-Koblin-513) Netflix also uses the tags to create recommendation micro-genres like "Goofy TV Shows" or "Girls Night In".[[512]](#cite_note-Koblin-513)

Netflix launched "responsive recommendations", which display titles on the home page based on what its users are actively searching, in 2025.[[513]](#cite_note-514)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Awards")]

Further information: [List of accolades received by Netflix](/wiki/List_of_accolades_received_by_Netflix "List of accolades received by Netflix")

On July 18, 2013, Netflix earned the first [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards") nominations for original [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_television "Streaming television") programs at the [65th Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/65th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "65th Primetime Emmy Awards"). Three of its series, *Arrested Development*, *[Hemlock Grove](/wiki/Hemlock_Grove_(TV_series) "Hemlock Grove (TV series)")* and *[House of Cards](/wiki/House_of_Cards_(American_TV_series) "House of Cards (American TV series)")*, earned a combined 14 nominations (nine for *House of Cards*, three for *Arrested Development* and two for *Hemlock Grove*).[[514]](#cite_note-NDWi2PEN-515) The *House of Cards* episode "[Chapter 1](/wiki/Chapter_1_(House_of_Cards) "Chapter 1 (House of Cards)")" received four nominations for both the 65th Primetime Emmy Awards and [65th Primetime Creative Arts Emmy Awards](/wiki/65th_Primetime_Creative_Arts_Emmy_Awards "65th Primetime Creative Arts Emmy Awards"), becoming the first episode of a streaming television series to receive a major Primetime Emmy Award nomination. With its win for Outstanding Cinematography for a Single-Camera Series, "Chapter 1" became the first episode from a streaming service to be awarded an Emmy.[[514]](#cite_note-NDWi2PEN-515)[[515]](#cite_note-516)[[516]](#cite_note-NMHWTPCAEA-517) David Fincher's win for Directing for a Drama Series for *House of Cards* made the episode the first from a streaming service to win a Primetime Emmy.[[517]](#cite_note-518)

On November 6, 2013, Netflix earned its first Grammy nomination when [You've Got Time](/wiki/You%27ve_Got_Time "You've Got Time") by [Regina Spektor](/wiki/Regina_Spektor "Regina Spektor")—the main title theme song for *Orange Is the New Black*—was nominated for Best Song Written for Visual Media.[[518]](#cite_note-519)

On December 12, 2013, the network earned six nominations for [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), including four for *House of Cards*.[[519]](#cite_note-520) Among those nominations was Wright for [Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Television Series Drama](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_%E2%80%93_Television_Series_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Television Series Drama") for her portrayal of Claire Underwood, which she won. With the accolade, Wright became the first actress to win a Golden Globe for a streaming television series. It also marked Netflix's first major acting award.[[520]](#cite_note-521)[[521]](#cite_note-522)[[522]](#cite_note-523) *House of Cards* and *Orange is the New Black* also won [Peabody Awards](/wiki/Peabody_Awards "Peabody Awards") in 2013.[[523]](#cite_note-524)

On January 16, 2014, Netflix became the first streaming service to earn an Academy Award nomination when *The Square* was nominated for Best Documentary Feature.[[524]](#cite_note-525)

On July 10, 2014, Netflix received 31 Emmy nominations. Among other nominations, *House of Cards* received nominations for [Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series"), Outstanding Directing in a Drama Series and Outstanding Writing in a Drama Series. Kevin Spacey and Robin Wright were nominated for Outstanding Lead Actor and Outstanding Lead Actress in a Drama Series. *Orange is the New Black* was nominated in the comedy categories, earning nominations for Outstanding Comedy Series, Outstanding Writing for a Comedy Series and Outstanding Directing for a Comedy Series. [Taylor Schilling](/wiki/Taylor_Schilling "Taylor Schilling"), [Kate Mulgrew](/wiki/Kate_Mulgrew "Kate Mulgrew"), and [Uzo Aduba](/wiki/Uzo_Aduba "Uzo Aduba") were respectively nominated for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Comedy Series, Outstanding Supporting Actress in a Comedy Series and Outstanding Guest Actress in a Comedy Series (the latter was for Aduba's recurring role in season one, as she was promoted to series regular for the show's second season).[[525]](#cite_note-526)

Netflix got the largest share of 2016 Emmy Award nominations, with 16 major nominations. However, streaming shows only got 24 nominations out of a total of 139, falling significantly behind cable. The 16 Netflix nominees were: *House of Cards* with Kevin Spacey, *A Very Murray Christmas* with Bill Murray, *Unbreakable Kimmy Schmidt*, *Master of None*, and *Bloodline*.[[526]](#cite_note-527)

*[Stranger Things](/wiki/Stranger_Things "Stranger Things")* received 19 nominations at the [2017 Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/2017_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "2017 Primetime Emmy Awards"), while *[The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown_(TV_series) "The Crown (TV series)")* received 13 nominations.[[527]](#cite_note-528)

In December 2017, Netflix was awarded [PETA](/wiki/PETA "PETA")'s Company of the Year for promoting animal rights movies and documentaries like *[Forks Over Knives](/wiki/Forks_Over_Knives "Forks Over Knives")* and *[What the Health](/wiki/What_the_Health "What the Health")*.[[528]](#cite_note-529)[[529]](#cite_note-530)

At the [90th Academy Awards](/wiki/90th_Academy_Awards "90th Academy Awards"), held on March 4, 2018, the film *[Icarus](/wiki/Icarus_(2017_film) "Icarus (2017 film)")*, distributed by Netflix, won its first [Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Documentary_Feature_Film "Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature Film"). During his remarks backstage, director and writer Bryan Fogel remarked that Netflix had "single-handedly changed the documentary world." *Icarus* had its premiere at the 2017 [Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/Sundance_Film_Festival "Sundance Film Festival") and was bought by Netflix for $5 million, one of the biggest deals ever for a non-fiction film.[[530]](#cite_note-531) Netflix became the network whose programs received more nomination at the 2018 [Primetime](/wiki/70th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "70th Primetime Emmy Awards") and [Creative Arts Emmy Awards](/wiki/70th_Primetime_Creative_Arts_Emmy_Awards "70th Primetime Creative Arts Emmy Awards") with 112 nominations, therefore breaking [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO")'s 17-years record as a network whose programs received more nomination at the Emmys, which received 108 nominations.[[531]](#cite_note-532)[[209]](#cite_note-:0-210)

On January 22, 2019, films distributed by Netflix scored 15 nominations for the [91st Academy Awards](/wiki/91st_Academy_Awards "91st Academy Awards"), including [Academy Award for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") for [Alfonso Cuarón](/wiki/Alfonso_Cuar%C3%B3n "Alfonso Cuarón")'s *[Roma](/wiki/Roma_(2018_film) "Roma (2018 film)")*, which was nominated for 10 awards.[[532]](#cite_note-533) The 15 nominations equal the total nominations films distributed by Netflix had received in previous years.

In 2020, Netflix received 20 TV nominations and films distributed by Netflix also got 22 film nominations at the [78th Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/78th_Golden_Globe_Awards "78th Golden Globe Awards"). It secured three out of the five nominations for [best drama TV series](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Television_Series_%E2%80%93_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Television Series – Drama") for *[The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown_(TV_series) "The Crown (TV series)")*, *[Ozark](/wiki/Ozark_(TV_series) "Ozark (TV series)")* and *[Ratched](/wiki/Ratched_(TV_series) "Ratched (TV series)")* and four of the five nominations for [best actress in a TV series](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_%E2%80%93_Television_Series_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Television Series Drama"): [Olivia Colman](/wiki/Olivia_Colman "Olivia Colman"), [Emma Corrin](/wiki/Emma_Corrin "Emma Corrin"), [Laura Linney](/wiki/Laura_Linney "Laura Linney") and [Sarah Paulson](/wiki/Sarah_Paulson "Sarah Paulson").[[533]](#cite_note-534)[[534]](#cite_note-535)

In 2020, Netflix earned 24 Academy Award nominations, marking the first time a streaming service led all studios.[[535]](#cite_note-536)

Films and programs distributed by Netflix received 30 nominations at the 2021 [Screen Actors Guild Awards](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Awards "Screen Actors Guild Awards"), more than any other distribution company, where their distributed films and programs won seven awards including best motion picture for *[The Trial of the Chicago 7](/wiki/The_Trial_of_the_Chicago_7 "The Trial of the Chicago 7")* and best TV drama for *[The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown_(TV_series) "The Crown (TV series)")*.[[536]](#cite_note-537)[[537]](#cite_note-538) Netflix also received the most nominations of any studio at the [93rd Academy Awards](/wiki/93rd_Academy_Awards "93rd Academy Awards")—35 total nominations with 7 award wins.[[538]](#cite_note-539)[[539]](#cite_note-540)

In February 2022, *[The Power of the Dog](/wiki/The_Power_of_the_Dog_(film) "The Power of the Dog (film)")*, a gritty western distributed by Netflix and directed by Jane Campion, received 12 nominations, including Best Picture, for the 94th annual Academy Awards. Films distributed by the streamer received a total of 72 nominations.[[540]](#cite_note-541) Campion became the third female to receive the Best Director award, winning her second Oscar for *The Power of the Dog*.[[541]](#cite_note-542) At the [50th International Emmy Awards](/wiki/50th_International_Emmy_Awards "50th International Emmy Awards"), Netflix original *[Sex Education](/wiki/Sex_Education_(TV_series) "Sex Education (TV series)")* won [Best Comedy Series](/wiki/International_Emmy_Award_for_Best_Comedy_Series "International Emmy Award for Best Comedy Series").[[542]](#cite_note-543) Later that year, Netflix received 26 Emmy Awards including six for *Squid Game*. The *Squid Game* wins for [Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series") and [Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series](/wiki/Outstanding_Directing_for_a_Drama_Series "Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series") were the first-ever for a non-English language series in those categories.[[543]](#cite_note-544)

In March 2023, Netflix won six Academy Awards including four for *[All Quiet on the Western Front](/wiki/All_Quiet_on_the_Western_Front_(2022_film) "All Quiet on the Western Front (2022 film)")* which was the most awarded Netflix film in its history. *[Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio](/wiki/Guillermo_del_Toro%27s_Pinocchio "Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio")* was the first streaming film to win [Best Animated Feature](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Animated_Feature "Academy Award for Best Animated Feature") and *[The Elephant Whisperers](/wiki/The_Elephant_Whisperers "The Elephant Whisperers")* was the first Indian-produced film to receive [Best Documentary Short Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Documentary_Short_Film "Academy Award for Best Documentary Short Film").[[544]](#cite_note-545) Netflix received 103 Emmy nominations including 13 each for the limited series [*Beef*](/wiki/Beef_(TV_series) "Beef (TV series)") *and [Dahmer – Monster: The Jeffrey Dahmer Story](/wiki/Dahmer_%E2%80%93_Monster:_The_Jeffrey_Dahmer_Story "Dahmer – Monster: The Jeffrey Dahmer Story").*[[545]](#cite_note-546)

In July 2024, Netflix received 107 Emmy nominations, which was the most of any network.[[546]](#cite_note-547)

In January 2025, Netflix received 18 Academy Award nominations across six titles—the most of any studio.[[547]](#cite_note-548) Thirteen of those nominations belonged to the musical crime drama *[Emilia Pérez](/wiki/Emilia_P%C3%A9rez "Emilia Pérez"),* making it the most nominated film of the year and the most nominated non-English language film in Academy history. *Emilia Pérez* ultimately won an award for [Best Original Song](/wiki/Best_Original_Song "Best Original Song") for "El Mal" and [Zoe Saldaña](/wiki/Zoe_Salda%C3%B1a "Zoe Saldaña") was awarded [Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress"). Other Netflix films recognized with nominations were *[The Only Girl in the Orchestra](/wiki/The_Only_Girl_in_the_Orchestra "The Only Girl in the Orchestra"),* which took home the award for Best Documentary Short, Pablo Larraín's *Maria*, Tyler Perry's *[The Six Triple Eight](/wiki/The_Six_Triple_Eight "The Six Triple Eight"),* animated feature *[Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl](/wiki/Wallace_%26_Gromit:_Vengeance_Most_Fowl "Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl"),* and live-action short film *[Anuja](/wiki/Anuja_(film) "Anuja (film)").*[[548]](#cite_note-549)

In July 2025, Netflix received 120 Emmy nominations, including 13 nods for *[Adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence_(TV_series) "Adolescence (TV series)")*, 11 for *[Monsters: The Lyle and Erik Menendez Story](/wiki/Monsters:_The_Lyle_and_Erik_Menendez_Story "Monsters: The Lyle and Erik Menendez Story")* and 10 for *Black Mirror*.[[549]](#cite_note-550) It won 30, including eight for [Outstanding Limited or Anthology Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Limited_or_Anthology_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Limited or Anthology Series") winner *[Adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence_(TV_series) "Adolescence (TV series)")*. The show's star, [Owen Cooper](/wiki/Owen_Cooper "Owen Cooper"), was the youngest Emmy winner ever in any male acting category at 15 years old.[[550]](#cite_note-551)[[551]](#cite_note-552)

In January 2026, Netflix received 18 Academy Award nominations across six titles. These included [Guillermo del Toro](/wiki/Guillermo_del_Toro "Guillermo del Toro")’s *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(2025_film) "Frankenstein (2025 film)")* with nine nominations (Best Picture, Best Supporting Actor, Best Original Score, Best Cinematography, Best Makeup and Hairstyling, Best Costume Design, Best Adapted Screenplay, Best Production Design, Best Sound)*,* [Clint Bentley](/wiki/Clint_Bentley "Clint Bentley")’s *[Train Dreams](/wiki/Train_Dreams_(film) "Train Dreams (film)")* with three nominations (Best Picture, Best Cinematography, Best Original Song)*,* *[The Perfect Neighbor](/wiki/The_Perfect_Neighbor "The Perfect Neighbor")* for Best Documentary, *[KPop Demon Hunters](/wiki/KPop_Demon_Hunters "KPop Demon Hunters")* for Best Animated Feature, as well as two short films: *[The Singers](/wiki/The_Singers "The Singers")* and *[All the Empty Rooms](/wiki/All_the_Empty_Rooms "All the Empty Rooms"),[[552]](#cite_note-553)* nominated for Live Action Short Film[[553]](#cite_note-554) and Documentary Short Film,[[554]](#cite_note-555) respectively.

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Netflix&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Main article: [Criticism of Netflix](/wiki/Criticism_of_Netflix "Criticism of Netflix")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Netflix_web_page_linked_from_the_%22Choose_a_New_Plan%22_button_in_the_Netflix_email_titled_%22Choose_a_new_plan_now_to_keep_watching%22%2C_2024.png/250px-Netflix_web_page_linked_from_the_%22Choose_a_New_Plan%22_button_in_the_Netflix_email_titled_%22Choose_a_new_plan_now_to_keep_watching%22%2C_2024.png)](/wiki/File:Netflix_web_page_linked_from_the_%22Choose_a_New_Plan%22_button_in_the_Netflix_email_titled_%22Choose_a_new_plan_now_to_keep_watching%22,_2024.png)

In 2024, Netflix discontinued its cheapest ad-free Basic plan. Users who were on the plan would have to pay extra for the next cheapest ad-free plan.

Customers of Netflix have complained about price increases in Netflix offerings dating back to the company's decision to separate its DVD rental and streaming services, which was quickly reversed. As Netflix increased its streaming output, it has faced calls to limit accessibility to [graphic violence](/wiki/Graphic_violence "Graphic violence") and include viewer advisories for issues such as sensationalism and promotion of [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience"). Netflix's content has also been criticized by [disability rights movement](/wiki/Disability_rights_movement "Disability rights movement") advocates for lack of [closed captioning](/wiki/Closed_captioning "Closed captioning") quality.[[555]](#cite_note-556) The launch of ad-supported plans has also been criticized by some subscribers as undermining Netflix's once ad-free experience.[[556]](#cite_note-557)

Some media organizations and competitors have criticized Netflix for selectively releasing ratings and [viewer numbers](/wiki/Audience_measurement "Audience measurement") of its original programming. The company has made claims boasting about viewership records without providing data to substantiate its successes or using problematic estimation methods.[[557]](#cite_note-558) In March 2020, some government agencies called for Netflix and other streamers to limit services due to increased broadband and energy consumption as use of the platform increased. In response, the company announced it would reduce [bit rate](/wiki/Bit_rate "Bit rate") across all streams in Europe, thus decreasing Netflix traffic on European networks by around 25 percent. These same steps were later taken in India.[[558]](#cite_note-559)

In May 2022, Netflix's shareholder Imperium Irrevocable Trust filed a lawsuit against the company for violating the US [securities](/wiki/Securities "Securities") laws.[[559]](#cite_note-560) In January 2024, a federal judge dismissed the suit, stating that shareholders failed to provide instances of Netflix lying about subscriber growth.[[560]](#cite_note-561)

In May 2023, Netflix officially banned the use of password sharing between individuals of different households, meaning sharing an account was only available to those living in the same house.[[443]](#cite_note-password_sharing-444)[[561]](#cite_note-562) After reporting a record number of new subscribers in Q4 2024, Netflix raised prices
in early 2025, and again in March 2026.[[562]](#cite_note-563)
